Also note its `herdr` seam module already queries `$HERDR_BIN_PATH` for "which workspaces have an active agent" to color its worktree-switcher (`W`) — if agent-team pushes per-worktree agent status somewhere herdr exposes it, these two plugins could interoperate (file-viewer showing agent-team's live status) rather than duplicate that query.

deep_dives.name: herdr-reviewr

It's the missing "structured feedback back into the agent" step he's presumably doing by hand-typing today, it's a native Rust TUI (zero LLM tokens spent rendering the diff — the diff never touches his coordinator's context, only the terse comment lines do), and the read-only PR tab via `gh` matches his GitHub-flow-first review culture. One sidebar per worktree pane maps 1:1 onto his existing git-worktree-per-worker setup.

(2) Manifest pattern in `herdr-plugin.toml`: `[[events]] on = "worktree.created"` for auto-reacting to lifecycle events, separate from `[[actions]]` (toggle/open/close) that are invoked either by a user keybind (bound in herdr's own config, not the manifest) or by another plugin via `herdr plugin action invoke`. deep_dives.name: herdr-wait
deep_dives.value: Directly automates the exact idle-vs-parked judgment call his own coordination rules make by hand ("an idle notification without a final report means stopped, not done"): when a god-agent-spawned Codex/opencode worker or a `herdr wait`-blocked pane goes idle, herdr-wait tags the sidebar row "waiting: codex" / "waiting: <target pane>" instead of a bare idle — so his run-board reads correctly at a glance across a fleet of Codex + Claude worker panes without him having to inspect process trees manually.
deep_dives.gotchas: Requires bun on PATH (already installed: bun 1.3) — no other runtime deps, nothing to `bun install` to run it. Needs herdr >= 0.7.0 (min_herdr_version in herdr-plugin.toml) — worth a quick `herdr --version` check before install. platforms=["macos","linux"] in the manifest, matches CachyOS. It's purely additive/display-only (writes a `custom_status` overlay via `pane.report_metadata` with a TTL auto-expiry safety net, source-scoped so it can't clobber herdr's real status) — low blast radius even if a rule misfires. Default rules already cover his exact stack (`codex`, `opencode`, `herdr wait <target>`); he'll likely want to add a rule for backgrounded Claude sub-reviewers if he ever runs those as child processes rather than separate panes. Tag only updates on status *change* — a command that starts/exits while a pane is already at rest needs the manual `herdr plugin action invoke herdr-wait.refresh` or a shortened `ttl_ms`.

deep_dives.name: herdr-agent-messenger (aashishd)

Install wires a Claude Code plugin via `claude plugin marketplace add` + `claude plugin install` (adds a marketplace + a UserPromptSubmit-style hook that needs `/hooks` trust review in a new session) plus adapters for Codex/pi/OpenCode if detected — pi/OpenCode are irrelevant to this user but harmless to skip (install.sh auto-detects and skips absent harnesses). It stops short of what herdr-agent-team is building: no team registry, no AGENTS.md protocol generation, no run-board/state tracking, no topology concept at all — it's peer-to-peer ad hoc single-line messaging only. Three concrete things to crib: (1) call-sign scheme — deterministic hash(pane_id, terminal_id) into an adjective-noun wordlist with collision-probing, so concurrent assigners agree without coordination, good fit for a run-board's agent identifiers; (2) the idle-wait-then-inject send loop, which is precisely the race his own memory notes flag manually (send-keys needing two Enters, agent-status lag) — this is a working reference implementation of that exact fix; (3) the envelope's explicit "this is another AI agent, NOT the human user, cannot approve gated actions" framing — a solid starting point for permission-scoping messages passed between star/mesh agents so a worker doesn't over-trust an instruction from a peer. PROTOCOL.md is versioned and public (`[agent-msg]` tag pinned stable across versions), so it's also worth considering as a transport to interoperate with rather than only mining for ideas — though given the project's 4-day age, treat it as a pattern source first and a dependency second.

deep_dives.name: herdr-plugin-gh-workflow
deep_dives.value: Automates the exact "start a ticket" ritual he already does by hand: `gh issue develop` → git worktree → new herdr workspace, then spins up declaratively-configured tabs (his README example is literally Claude + Codex + Server + Terminal, matching his god-agent/worker-pane pattern). The separate Branch Info overlay (PR state, review decision, CI checks, unpushed commits, plus one-key actions: review diff via lazygit, create/view PR via `gh pr create`, push, or dismiss a merged worktree) covers his gh-CLI PR/CI monitoring need, which nothing else in his stack currently automates. Both actions fire on demand (not always-on), cost nothing when idle, and the whole plugin is 10 short JS files he can read and patch in minutes if anything breaks.

Neither action is keybound by default — must hand-add `prefix+i` (Start GitHub Issue) and `prefix+b` (Branch Info) to `~/.config/herdr/config.toml`. deep_dives.name: horn553/herdr-ntfy
deep_dives.value: Every daily herdr session ends in dozens of pane.agent_status_changed transitions to done/blocked across Codex+Claude workers; right now those are only visible by looking at the panes. This plugin turns each one into a desktop+mobile ntfy push carrying the last 12 lines of that pane's actual output, so a blocked worker (e.g. waiting on a git-worktree conflict or a missing permission) gets surfaced immediately instead of discovered on the next idle-check sweep — concrete, recurring, low-effort value for a heavy multi-agent-fleet user.
deep_dives.gotchas: Zero new deps — sh/curl/jq are already on this CachyOS box (curl 8.21, jq 1.8.2). Setup is a `.env` with NTFY_URL/TOKEN/TITLE/LINES, chmod 600, done in under a minute; ntfy.sh is free and self-hostable so there's no vendor lock-in (matches the triage note vs. an email-based alternative). Platforms = linux+macos (toml confirms Linux support explicitly, not just "any POSIX shell" inference). Requires herdr >=0.7.0 — worth a one-line version check before install. Important scope limit: this is PUSH-ONLY notification, not the "remote approval of blocked agents" half of the user's stated want — there's no reply/action-button path back into herdr (ntfy does support action buttons in general, but this plugin doesn't wire any), so it satisfies the notification need but not the approval-loop need. Maintenance signal is thin-but-fresh: 1 star, 0 open issues, single shell file, likely solo author's own itch-scratch built alongside herdr 0.7.0's event-hook feature — but the code is ~250 lines of readable POSIX sh with no external runtime, so even if it goes stale it's trivially forkable/patchable in place.

deep_dives.name: herdr-attention (milkyskies)
deep_dives.value: Binds one keypress (prefix+a) to jump focus to the next agent that's blocked-on-you or done-and-unreviewed, cycling across every workspace/tab/pane and skipping the one you're already on — exactly the "find the fleet member that needs me" primitive for a god-agent workflow running many concurrent Codex/Claude worker panes, replacing manual sidebar-hunting with a single keybind.
deep_dives.gotchas: Pure Node.js (no build step), platforms = linux/macos/windows so Linux/CachyOS is fine; requires herdr >= 0.7.0 (confirm your installed version supports `agent focus` and the `agent_status`/`focused` fields on `agent list`). Keybind isn't automatic — you must add the `[[keys.command]]` block yourself to `~/.config/herdr/config.toml` and run `herdr server reload-config`; the manifest only ships a suggestion. Detection quality is herdr-core's, not the plugin's: it only marks an agent `blocked` when the on-screen prompt matches a known approval/question/permission UI pattern — anything unrecognized falls back to `idle` and gets silently skipped. Worth a quick live test specifically on your Codex panes, since your own notes already record herdr-agent-targeting quirks with Codex TUIs (label lookup fails, submits need two Enters) — those are a different code path but suggest Codex-pane detection in herdr is imperfect enough to spot-check before trusting the cycle to catch every blocked Codex worker. No persisted cursor state (by design — relies on `done` clearing on focus and you clearing `blocked` yourself), so if you want "only cycle attached workspaces" you'd fork and filter on `workspace_id` yourself (README flags this as a 2-line edit in jump.js).
deep_dives.maintenance: Created and pushed 2026-07-08, single initial commit only (6 days old as of 2026-07-14), 0 open issues, 1 star — too new to have a maintenance track record; not stale, just unproven. Whole repo is 5 files / ~60 lines of JS, trivially auditable and forkable if it goes unmaintained.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Complementary, not competing: herdr-attention is a thin consumer of herdr-core's existing `agent list`/`agent focus` primitives (navigation/UX), while herdr-agent-team is an orchestration engine (spawn, AGENTS.md protocol generation, star/mesh topology, run-board state, event push) — no functional overlap. Two things worth stealing for herdr-agent-team: (1) the exact JSON shape of `herdr agent list` this plugin relies on (`{result: {agents: [{pane_id, agent, agent_status, focused}]}}`) is a de-facto schema reference for building your own run-board state without re-deriving it by trial and error; (2) the priority-queue pattern itself — `PRIORITY = {blocked: 0, done: 1}`, sort, skip-currently-focused, cycle-until-empty, fire a `herdr notification show` toast via best-effort try/catch — is a clean ~15-line reference implementation for the "push status events to god pane" feature you're building; herdr-agent-team's run-board could offer the same "jump to next blocked/done in MY team" as a scoped action (filtered to your team's pane set rather than every agent in herdr), which is a concrete differentiator over installing this plugin standalone. Also note the manifest structure (`herdr-plugin.toml` with `[[actions]]` + `[[keys.command]]`, `contexts = ["workspace"]`, command as an argv array) is a minimal real-world example of the plugin packaging format your own plugin will need to ship.

deep_dives.name: herdr-active-agent-jump
deep_dives.value: Adds `next`/`previous` actions that hop focus only through panes currently `working` or `blocked`, in stable pane-id order, wrapping at the ends — exactly the "walk my live fleet" primitive the coordinator loop needs when several Codex/Claude worker panes are in flight at once. Confirmed on this machine: herdr 0.7.3 is installed and has the exact `agent list`/`agent focus`/`plugin` subcommands the plugin's ~120-line jump.js calls, so it isn't guessing at a stale API.
deep_dives.gotchas: Ships with NO default keybinding — you must add two `[[keys.command]]` entries to ~/.config/herdr/config.toml and run `herdr server reload-config`; if you bind prefix+j/prefix+k you must also blank herdr's default focus_pane_down/up to avoid a conflict (README calls this out). Ordering is by pane_id, not visual/layout position, so after reshuffling panes the "lap" order can feel non-spatial. It inherits herdr's own blocked-detection heuristic (only flags `blocked` when herdr recognizes the on-screen prompt as an approval/question UI) — worth a quick smoke test that your Codex panes actually flip to `blocked` and not `idle`/`unknown` before relying on it. Requires Node (present: node 26) and herdr >=0.7.0 (present: 0.7.3); zero other deps, no build step.

deep_dives.name: Feasy01/herdr-allow
deep_dives.value: Directly hits your worktree-per-worker pattern: every fresh Codex/Claude worktree herdr spins up gets your gitignored .env/API-key/local-config files copied in automatically (via worktree.created/worktree.opened hooks), instead of you noticing mid-task that a worker's checkout can't run because secrets never got copied off main. One .herdr-allow allowlist file per repo, default non-destructive/idempotent, with a manual `herdr plugin action invoke herdr-allow.copy` resync and per-plugin `overwrite=true` opt-in — recurring weekly-or-more pain solved for near-zero cost.
deep_dives.gotchas: Runtime deps (bash, git, jq-or-python3) are all already on your CachyOS box — no install burden, no compiled toolchain, pure Shell (185-line copy-allowed.sh). herdr-plugin.toml explicitly lists `platforms = ["linux", "macos"]`, so Linux is a first-class target, not an afterthought. One caveat baked into the README itself: `**` globs need bash >=4 (irrelevant to you on Arch, called out only because stock macOS bash 3.2 chokes on it). Zero external engagement (0 stars, 0 issues ever opened) — read the 185-line script yourself once before trusting it with secrets; it's short enough to fully audit in a few minutes, and it does have a tests/run.sh covering event-mode, fallback source discovery, skip/overwrite semantics, and path-escape rejection.
deep_dives.maintenance: Single commit, pushed once on 2026-07-02 and never touched since (12 days old at time of review); 0 stars, 0 open issues, not archived — reads as a solo weekend build with zero track record, not an actively maintained project. Low risk given the code is tiny/auditable and does one narrow job, but don't expect upstream bug-fix responsiveness if you hit an edge case.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Narrow but concrete overlap with the "worktree automation" facet of herdr-agent-team (not the orchestration/notification/run-board facets — this plugin has none of that). Worth lifting directly: (1) the manifest shape itself — `[[events]] on = "worktree.created"/"worktree.opened", command = [...]` plus `[[actions]] contexts = ["workspace"]` for a manual re-invoke, and `min_herdr_version` pinning — is a clean reference for how your own plugin should declare its worktree-provisioning hooks (e.g. auto-dropping the generated AGENTS.md protocol file or shared team credentials into every new worker worktree the same way). (2) Its source-discovery fallback chain (checkout the worktree came from → main worktree via first entry of `git worktree list`) is exactly the kind of logic you'll need if herdr-agent-team ever needs to seed new worker worktrees with shared state. (3) Security pattern worth copying: explicit allowlist + rejection of path-escaping entries (absolute, `~`, `..`) so a provisioning plugin can't be tricked into spreading files outside the repo. (4) Default non-destructive/idempotent copy with an explicit `overwrite=true` opt-in via `herdr plugin config-dir <name>/config` is a good idempotency pattern for any "sync state into every worktree" action your plugin runs on worker (re)spawn. Nothing here informs star/mesh topology, status-push-to-god-pane, or run-board state — those remain yours to design.

deep_dives.gotchas: Manifest hard-gates `platforms = ["macos"]` in herdr-plugin.toml — this isn't just the hotkey daemon, it's the whole plugin's declared platform, and the README's Requirements section confirms it explicitly: "macOS (uses osascript and Carbon)". The always-executed `bin/focus` path (not only the optional hotkey) shells out to `osascript -e 'tell application ... to activate'` to raise the terminal app — meaningless/absent on KWin+Wayland, no kdotool/wmctrl/qdbus fallback shipped. The pane-cycling logic itself (`herdr agent list` → jq filter on `agent_status` blocked>done → `herdr agent focus`) is plain bash/jq and would run fine on Linux, but the manifest's platform declaration will very likely block `herdr plugin install` from even installing it on CachyOS. Hotkey daemon additionally needs Xcode CLT/swiftc + Carbon + a macOS LaunchAgent — 100% inapplicable. Solo author, all commit messages in Japanese, no tests/CI (.github absent).
deep_dives.maintenance: Very young (created 2026-07-06, ~8 days old), 2 PRs merged solo by trapple through 2026-07-09, then quiet 5 days (as of 2026-07-14) — 0 open issues, 0 stars, no releases, no CI: too little history to call "maintained," reads as a personal utility open-sourced mid-build, not yet a community project.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Adjacent to, not competing with, herdr-agent-team — herdr-focus is a single-purpose "jump to next pane needing attention" convenience action, not an orchestration/spawn/topology tool, so no product overlap. Three small patterns worth stealing: (1) the `herdr agent list` JSON schema (`agent_status`, `pane_id`, `focused`) plus a status-priority queue (blocked>done) with round-robin cycling off the currently-focused pane — directly reusable for a run-board "focus next agent needing attention" companion action; (2) the single-action-multiple-triggers architecture ("global hotkey → daemon → plugin action invoke; herdr keybinding → same action; manual CLI → same action" — one action, zero duplicated logic) is a clean precedent for wiring herdr-agent-team's own status-push events without triplicating handlers; (3) the manifest's `[[panes]] placement = "overlay"` trick for streaming live build/setup progress inside herdr (used here for the hotkey-daemon build) is reusable for streaming herdr-agent-team's own team-scaffold/worktree-provisioning progress. Cautionary lesson to differentiate on: herdr-focus scoped itself to `platforms = ["macos"]` at the manifest root even though most of its logic is portable — herdr-agent-team should declare cross-platform support explicitly (or omit the field) so Linux users aren't locked out by a manifest default, and should build any "raise window" behavior on a portable abstraction (or skip it) rather than hardcoding osascript.

deep_dives.name: herdr-worktree-provisioner
deep_dives.value: Automates exactly the "worktree fan-out pre-flight" he currently does by hand per his own environment notes — ghostty symlink, `git update-index --skip-worktree`, restoring build deps/toolchain — by firing a per-repo setup command automatically in every new worktree's own pane the moment it's created, deduped so it never double-runs. For limux specifically he can commit `.herdr/worktree-provisioner.json` at the repo root so the symlink+skip-worktree dance ships with the branch instead of living only in his memory/notes.
deep_dives.gotchas: Only fires on `worktree.created`/`workspace.created` emitted by `herdr worktree create` (or a launcher built on it) — herdr's own UI "new worktree" button emits just `workspace.focused` and is silently NOT auto-provisioned, so confirm which path he actually uses before relying on it. Deps are just bash + jq (both already on this box) plus herdr>=0.7.0 — no node/cargo/build step; the whole plugin is a single 14KB, fully-readable shell script, trivial to audit or rip out. One real risk: a repo-committed `.herdr/worktree-provisioner.json` executes its setup list directly with no trust gate yet (ROADMAP.md documents a planned direnv-style allow-list, not shipped) — fine for limux/his own repos, but don't let repo-local config run for third-party repos he doesn't control until that lands; use the central config there instead.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very young: created 2026-07-02, 4 commits over its first 6 days (last push 2026-07-08, so 6 days quiet as of today), 0 stars, 0 open issues, not archived. No community track record to lean on, but the commit history itself shows real care — a same-day fix for a double-provision race condition via an atomic `mkdir` claim, and a self-authored ROADMAP.md that proactively flags the plugin's own security gap rather than hiding it.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Genuine overlap: it's a herdr event-hook plugin exercising the same `worktree.created`/`workspace.created` events and pane-targeting concerns herdr-agent-team will need for per-worker provisioning. Three patterns worth lifting directly: (1) target the event payload's `root_pane` (or workspace's first pane) explicitly, never "the focused pane" — this is precisely what lets it compose with a launcher instead of racing it, and agent-team's status-push/injection needs the same discipline so it never types into a worker's own agent pane; (2) atomic dedup via `mkdir` claim keyed by sha1(path), with inode tracking to tell "restarted" apart from "deleted and recreated at the same path" — directly reusable for "spawn team once per workspace" idempotency; (3) it re-queries `herdr workspace get $wsid` as source of truth instead of trusting the event payload's cached fields — a pattern agent-team's run-board should copy for status truth rather than trusting stale event data. Where to differentiate: this plugin deliberately stays single-purpose and explicitly punts layout/launching to other plugins ("composes instead of competing") — worth treating as an architectural precedent to keep herdr-agent-team's orchestration/notification/run-board pieces as separable event-reactive units internally, even if they ship as one plugin.

deep_dives.name: herdr-whistle
deep_dives.value: Solves exactly the stated gap: a Telegram bot gated to your owner_id that lists/reads/sends/closes/starts herdr agents from your phone. watcher.go polls `herdr agent list` every 5s, diffs status per (pane_id, session UUID) to catch idle→blocked transitions, and — the standout feature — choices.go parses @clack/prompts and box-drawing selection menus out of the pane's scrollback and turns them into a Telegram inline keyboard, so a worker (Claude/Codex) stuck on a menu can be answered with a tap instead of you typing the exact text back in. Given how many worker panes you run daily and how often you're away from the desk, this directly shortens your "agent sat blocked for an hour" tail.
deep_dives.gotchas: Linux is explicitly supported (herdr-plugin.toml platforms=["linux","macos"]) and it's a single static Go binary — go.mod wants go1.26.4, you have 1.26.5, so `go build -o herdr-whistle .` then `herdr plugin link` just works, no cargo/node/python/docker involved. Real setup cost is outside the code: you need your own Telegram bot token (BotFather) and your numeric Telegram user ID hand-edited into config.toml — no wizard. No LICENSE file in the repo (fine for local personal use, would matter if you ever forked/redistributed). No .github/workflows — CI is `go test ./...` run by hand, though 5 _test.go files plus an AGENTS.md with documented test quirks (mocked package-level vars, 8-13s timing windows) suggest the author actually runs them. Confirm your herdr install is >=0.7.0 (manifest's min_herdr_version) before linking. `/send` executes via `herdr pane run` (types text + Enter) rather than raw key injection — matches your CLAUDE.local.md note that raw key injection (wtype/ydotool) is broken on your KWin/Wayland setup, so this sidesteps a known trap rather than hitting it.
deep_dives.maintenance: Created 2026-07-02, one focused 3-day burst of 16 commits (scaffold → bot → watcher → choice-parser → review fixes → tests → autostart spec) ending 2026-07-04, then quiet for ~10 days as of today (2026-07-14) — young and unstarred (0 stars, 0 open issues) but not stale, and the burst shipped a complete, tested feature set rather than stopping mid-build. Author (amurru / Ammar Zerouk, 55 followers, 37 public repos, account since 2011) is a prolific solo dev, not a first-time or drive-by committer — code quality (flock-based single-instance daemon with documented TOCTOU tolerance, goroutine-decoupled notification queue with drop+log on overflow, 30s timeouts on every herdr CLI subprocess call, HTML-escaped Telegram output, owner-only auth) reads well above typical 0-star weekend-project bar.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Complementary, not competing — herdr-whistle is single-purpose (Telegram remote control) and does no team spawning, AGENTS.md generation, or topology work, so it doesn't collide with herdr-agent-team's mission. Three patterns worth stealing for herdr-agent-team's own "push status events to god pane" and run-board features: (1) watcher.go's poll-and-diff loop keyed on `{PaneID, SessionID}` (SessionID = agent_session UUID) cleanly handles a pane being reused by a new agent instance after restart — the same dedup problem your run-board state will hit; (2) choices.go's dual-format parser (@clack/prompts prompts, box-drawing menus) detects when a worker pane is showing a structured decision rather than a generic "blocked" — reuse that signal to render "needs decision: N options" in your run-board instead of a flat blocked flag, and route it through your star/mesh topology for auto-answer or human-in-the-loop; (3) lifecycle.go's autostart pattern (state dir via os.UserConfigDir, non-blocking flock, detached worker spawn, wired to herdr's workspace.created/workspace.focused events, idempotent no-op if already running) is a clean template if herdr-agent-team ever needs its own always-on coordinator daemon rather than living entirely inside the god pane's process.

Requires herdr>=0.7.0 and `gh auth login`. No default keybinding — must hand-bind `prefix+alt+g` (or similar) in ~/.config/herdr/config.toml himself. Ctrl+click-to-open-detail depends on herdr's mouse layer seeing the click before the host terminal's own URL handler grabs it — README flags this as a known failure mode on Windows Terminal; unverified whether his terminal (konsole/foot/whatever he runs herdr panes in on KDE Wayland) swallows it first — worth a 30-second live check before relying on it. Repo intentionally has NOT added the `herdr-plugin` marketplace topic yet ("avoid an empty repo on the marketplace until it works") per its own dev notes, i.e. the single maintainer treats it as not-yet-fully-proven; mutating actions (close/reopen/edit-labels/comment) touch real GitHub state, so worth a dry run in a throwaway repo first given only 3 days of code history.

(2) internal/herdrctx/herdrctx.go is a ~20-line directly-reusable pattern for resolving workspace/pane cwd from HERDR_PLUGIN_CONTEXT_JSON (falls back workspace_cwd -> focused_pane_cwd) — exactly the context-resolution herdr-agent-team needs when a pane action fires. (3) Its docs/plugin-development-notes.md documents the full injected-env-var contract (HERDR_SOCKET_PATH, HERDR_BIN_PATH, HERDR_PLUGIN_ID/ROOT/CONFIG_DIR/STATE_DIR, HERDR_WORKSPACE_ID/TAB_ID/PANE_ID, HERDR_PLUGIN_ACTION_ID/EVENT/CLICKED_URL) plus notes that herdr's event-hook list isn't documented anywhere and had to be discovered empirically — a gap herdr-agent-team will hit too. Most importantly, that same notes doc names three sibling repos as closer precedent than github-dash and worth a follow-up look before building further: ogulcancelik/herdr-plugin-examples (contains `agent-telegram-notify` — an event-hook-to-external-notification pattern matching his desktop/mobile-approval goal directly), ogulcancelik/herdr-plugin-github-start (launches Codex/Claude from a GitHub issue/PR — manifest precedent for agent-launch actions), and persiyanov/herdr-reviewr (a TUI sidebar for reviewing an agent's diff changes — overlaps his "code review of agent diffs" and run-board territory more than github-dash does). Too young for community signal, but the code itself is defensively engineered: fcntl-locked atomic state writes, a correctly-dispatching dual tmux/herdr pane backend, and a ~48KB test file against a 78KB source file — better maintained-looking than the star count suggests.

(2) Its `is_herdr_target()` dispatch (pane ids shaped `wB:pN` route to `herdr pane get/read/run`, everything else falls back to tmux) is the exact fix for the `agent_not_found`-by-label gotcha already hit and logged in this user's own memory — herdr-agent-team's worker addressing should adopt the same id-shape detection. (3) Its reply-delivery loop (send → poll pane capture once/sec up to 2 min, classify pending/registered/processed, clear-and-retype on stall, warn at 15s and timeout) is the missing "confirm the god-pane's routed command actually landed" piece herdr-agent-team needs for its star/mesh topology — right now nothing in his stack closes that loop. (4) Its ticket-id + thread-per-pane routing (collision-avoided short ids, one Slack thread or Telegram forum-topic per exact window/pane) is a ready template for disambiguating N concurrent worker status streams on one control surface. Differentiate rather than compete: this plugin owns the external chat channel (Telegram/Slack as remote control plane); herdr-agent-team's run-board is presumably local/in-terminal, so the better long-term move is to have herdr-agent-team emit the same event shape this bridge already consumes, letting it act as the mobile/remote leg instead of herdr-agent-team building its own bot plumbing.

deep_dives.name: herdr-layout-tools
deep_dives.value: Solves a friction this user hits routinely as a heavy multi-pane herdr coordinator: lopsided splits after closing a worker pane (equalize, prefix+shift+e) and promoting one pane to a big "main" view (reshape main-left / main-left+grid, prefix+m / prefix+shift+m) — both in-place via a scratch-tab hop that preserves tab id, pane ids, and live processes, which is exactly what a god-agent holding pane/tab id bookkeeping needs. Also ships a Claude skill (fix-herdr-layout) so the coordinator agent itself can trigger a reshape from natural language ("make the editor main") instead of the user doing it by hand.
deep_dives.gotchas: Both prereqs already satisfied on this machine: herdr 0.7.3 ≥ required 0.7.0, node v26.4.0 on PATH. No package.json/npm deps — plain node built-ins (net, child_process) calling the herdr CLI + raw socket JSON-RPC for layout.export (which has no CLI surface). Platforms explicitly list linux+macos, so this is not a macOS-only tool. Install is two steps: `herdr plugin install edouard-andrei/herdr-layout-tools` (or link a local clone) PLUS manually adding 3 `[[keys.command]]` blocks to `~/.config/herdr/config.toml` (keybindings are not auto-wired) then `herdr server reload-config`. Keys are chosen to avoid herdr defaults (prefix+shift+e, prefix+m, prefix+shift+m) so no rebind conflicts expected.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very new: created and all 4 commits pushed within one ~18-minute burst on 2026-07-08 (6 days old as of today), then quiet — no activity since. 0 stars, 0 open issues, not archived, single apparent contributor. Feature-complete/small-surface (3 .mjs files) rather than actively iterated; test.mjs only covers pure planning logic, no e2e/CI harness, so a future herdr CLI/socket contract change wouldn't be caught automatically — but the codebase is small enough (~150 lines across herdr.mjs/reshape.mjs/equalize.mjs) that this user could self-patch if it broke, consistent with his own plugin-building habits.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No functional overlap with herdr-agent-team (this plugin does pure pane/tab layout reshaping — no spawning, no AGENTS.md generation, no status-event pushing, no run-board, no mesh/star topology logic). But there is a directly reusable pattern worth stealing: the "scratch-tab hop" technique (park non-main panes in a throwaway tab via two live `pane move`s each, then replay them back into the *original* tab in the target shape) is the only way found so far to change pane topology while keeping tab id + pane ids stable and processes alive — `layout.apply` recreates panes (new ids, restarted processes) and same-tab `pane move` is a no-op. If herdr-agent-team ever needs to visually rearrange a spawned team's panes (e.g. laying a star/mesh topology into a grid, or rebalancing after a worker exits) without invalidating the god agent's held pane/tab ids, this is the technique to reuse rather than reinvent. Also worth copying: the CLI-for-mutations + raw-socket-for-reads split in herdr.mjs (layout.export is socket-only), and the documented `--ratio` calibration gotcha (herdr's ratio = the first/left/top child's fraction, not the new pane's) — a subtlety that would silently mirror any layout code herdr-agent-team writes itself.

deep_dives.name: herdr-hunk (JacquesvanWyk/herdr-hunk)
deep_dives.value: Autodiff hooks pane.agent_status_changed and auto-opens a live, unfocused hunk.dev diff split beside any tracked Claude/Codex worker pane the instant it goes idle with uncommitted changes — automating the "review agent diffs" step of his god-agent/worktree loop with zero manual invocation. The fzf picker (working tree/staged/commit/range/branch-vs-upstream/stash, session-aware so it never stacks duplicate panes) also covers his ad-hoc diff-review needs for limux itself.
deep_dives.gotchas: Needs hunk (modem-dev/hunk, 6.8k stars, pushed today, TypeScript/Node) which isn't installed on this box — `npm i -g hunkdiff` lands it in ~/.local/bin, which matches this machine's npm prefix, so autodiff.sh's exported PATH picks it up automatically. fzf and jq are already present; herdr 0.7.3 exceeds the min_herdr_version 0.7.0 the plugin declares. No LICENSE file on the repo (fine for personal use, matters only if redistributing). autodiff.sh's change-dedup signature shells out to `shasum` — present here only via perl-core (`/usr/bin/core_perl/shasum`), not guaranteed on a minimal Arch install, worth a one-line `command -v shasum` check if it ever moves machines. Platforms declared: linux + macos (no Windows) — matches this box.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very fresh: repo created 2026-07-11, last push 2026-07-12 (3 days old at review time), 0 stars, 0 open issues — no real track record yet. Mitigated by size: ~230 lines of plain, fully-readable bash + one TOML manifest (audited in full above — no network calls, no eval of untrusted input, defensive early-exits on every missing dependency). Same author shipped three sibling herdr plugins (herdr-lazygit, herdr-keys, herdr-linear) in the same 2-3 day window, signaling active, ongoing engagement with the herdr plugin ecosystem rather than a one-off drop.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No scope collision with herdr-agent-team (that's orchestration/topology/AGENTS.md protocol; this is single-purpose diff viewing) but three implementation patterns are directly reusable: (1) event-hook wiring — `[[events]] on = "pane.agent_status_changed"` plus parsing `HERDR_PLUGIN_EVENT_JSON` via jq for `.data.agent_status` / `.data.pane_id` is a concrete worked example of the exact event herdr-agent-team will want to consume (or emit compatibly) for its own status-push-to-god-pane mechanism. (2) idempotent re-trigger suppression — hashing repo/worktree state (tracked diff + untracked file contents) into a file under `HERDR_PLUGIN_STATE_DIR` so the same event doesn't refire until state actually changed is directly applicable to run-board update-vs-noise control. (3) pane-injection + config idioms — `herdr plugin pane open --target-pane <id> --placement split --direction right --no-focus` for dropping a companion pane beside a specific worker without stealing focus, and `herdr plugin config-dir <id>` + a flag-file for a cheap on/off toggle, both map cleanly onto a run-board pane and a "mute status pushes" switch.

deep_dives.name: herdr-remote (dcolinmorgan)
deep_dives.value: Gives phone-based read/approve/interrupt/Ctrl-C for blocked Codex/Claude worker panes when away from the desk — directly serving the "remote approval of blocked agents" want. The relevant path on his Linux box is NOT the headline macOS menu-bar app but the Telegram bot + local relay combo, which needs no tunnel (both processes make outbound-only connections) and delivers the same one-tap approve/trust/interrupt UX from a phone.
deep_dives.gotchas: Headline "10-second install" (Herdi.app, Swift menu-bar app) is macOS-only and useless on KDE/CachyOS. Real Linux support does exist and looks deliberate — herdr-plugin.toml declares platforms=["macos","linux"], install-service.sh has an explicit uname-based branch and Linux log-dir fallback (/var/log or ~/.local/state) — but it is NOT a one-line `herdr plugin install`: the README's own instructions are to install the tiny companion plugin `herdr-push` (pure shell, zero deps) for the event hook, then separately git-clone herdr-remote and hand-run `relay/start.sh` or `uv run relay/herdr_telegram.py`. Needs Python 3.10+ with `uv` (available via pacman) and, for the phone-web/tunnel path, `cloudflared` (AUR); the Telegram-only path skips both tunnel and web app entirely. Relay auth (HERDR_RELAY_TOKEN) is optional — must be set manually before exposing via any tunnel, since an unauthenticated relay lets anyone send keystrokes into a live agent session. Repo already shipped one real security bug (Telegram bot commands not restricted to the owner's CHAT_ID) patched same day it was reported — reassuring responsiveness, but also evidence of a still-green codebase. One recent commit swapped a `setup-linux.sh` for an oddly named `.pi-kiro-auth.js` with no clear description — worth a skim before trusting the Linux install flow blindly.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very active — pushed today (2026-07-14) with a same-day security fix; but the repo is only ~3 weeks old (created 2026-06-21), solo maintainer (dcolinmorgan), 62 stars, 2 open issues. Fast-moving and responsive, not yet battle-tested.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Directly relevant infra patterns for herdr-agent-team's "push status events to god pane" / run-board leg: (1) the event-hook contract itself — herdr-plugin.toml `[[events]] on = "pane.agent_status_changed"` fires a subprocess with the event as JSON in `HERDR_PLUGIN_EVENT_JSON`; herdr-remote's own hook (relay/on_event.py) just UDP-forwards that to a local aggregator — cheap, decoupled, worth copying verbatim as the "core always-on, zero-dep" half of a heavier optional consumer (their split: thin herdr-push plugin vs. heavier Python relay). (2) Belt-and-suspenders state sync — the relay both consumes the real-time push AND independently polls `herdr pane list --json` / `herdr pane read --lines N --source recent` every 2s, so missed/late events self-heal; a run-board should do the same rather than trusting push-only. (3) Approval-prompt extraction — regex-based parsing of raw pane text (TOOL_OPTIONS/SUBAGENT_OPTIONS constants + a CHROME_RE that strips box-drawing/spinner noise) to detect "blocked" state and recover the actual option menu from unstructured terminal output — a genuinely reusable technique since herdr agents don't expose that structurally. (4) Multi-host fan-out via a plain `HERDR_REMOTES` comma-list wrapping every herdr CLI call in `ssh -o BatchMode=yes host herdr ...` — a low-tech precedent for star/mesh topology across machines worth comparing against whatever transport herdr-agent-team picks. Where to differentiate, not imitate: herdr-remote is single-operator monitor/approve (human approves one agent at a time via dashboard/phone/Telegram) with no concept of a team AGENTS.md protocol, agent-to-agent mesh messaging, or task/run assignment — that's the entire domain herdr-agent-team is adding, so there's no feature competition, only infrastructure patterns to borrow for the status-push/approval-surface piece.

deep_dives.name: herdr-spreader (yuk1ty)
deep_dives.value: A tmuxinator-for-herdr: declare workspaces/tabs/nested-pane-splits/env/startup-commands once in YAML, `herdr-spreader apply` rebuilds the whole layout deterministically (IDs threaded through real herdr CLI responses, not guessed). Genuine value if the user has a fixed daily dev layout for a given project (e.g. limux: nvim + `cargo watch -x test` split + server pane) he re-creates by hand each session — this replaces that ritual with one command. It does NOT touch agent orchestration, AGENTS.md generation, status events, or team topology at all, so it is not a competitor to herdr-agent-team's actual purpose; it's a layer below it (pane/tab provisioning only).
deep_dives.gotchas: Pure Rust: `cargo build --release` via `herdr-plugin.toml`'s `[[build]]`, no node/bun/python. `herdr-plugin.toml` explicitly lists `platforms = ["linux", "macos"]` and `min_herdr_version = "0.7.0"` — confirm the user's herdr install meets that floor before installing. Config is a **static** YAML file (every workspace/tab/pane enumerated literally) with no loop/templating construct — fine for a fixed personal IDE-style layout, but a poor fit for spawning a variable-size, dynamically-decided agent team, since the pane count would need hand-editing per run rather than being generated from "N workers this session." Young project (v0.1.1, first commits early July 2026) but pushed 2 days ago (2026-07-12), 1 open issue, has unit+integration tests (mock backend + fake herdr binary fixture) and a clippy/fmt-gated CI — healthy hygiene for its size, just not battle-tested yet.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very fresh and active (pushed 2026-07-12, i.e. 2 days before evaluation; v0.1.1 bugfix release 2026-07-07), single small repo, 1 open issue, not archived — low-risk to revisit later but too young to call "battle-tested."
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Overlaps with the `workspace-manager` candidate the user is also triaging (which auto-applies layouts per-worktree) — per triage's own note, pick one rather than run both, since both solve "get N panes into the right shape on demand." herdr-spreader is the more general/reusable of the two designs for that narrow slice, and has three patterns worth stealing directly for herdr-agent-team: (1) **focus-at-creation, not focus-after** — herdr v0.7.0 has no `pane.focus` socket method and the CLI's focus command only does directional navigation, so herdr-spreader avoids a whole class of "focus landed on the wrong pane" bugs by passing `--focus`/`--no-focus` on every `workspace create`/`tab create`/`pane split` call at the moment the target pane is created, rather than trying to re-target it afterward — directly applicable when god-agent spawns a team and wants the coordinator pane focused deterministically. (2) **`wait_for.match` + `timeout_ms` gating per pane** — exactly the "provision worker panes in dependency order" idea triage flagged; herdr-agent-team could reuse this shape for e.g. not injecting a worker's first brief until its `git worktree add` + `cd` prompt has printed, instead of a blind sleep. (3) **strict YAML validation (reject unknown keys at parse time)** — worth copying for AGENTS.md-adjacent config files so typos in a team-topology spec fail loud, not silently. None of this reaches into agent semantics (no AGENTS.md, no status-push, no run-board, no mesh/star topology) — that part of herdr-agent-team has no prior art here and remains fully the user's own design space.

deep_dives.name: herdr-worktrunk
deep_dives.value: Wires the mature, popular `worktrunk` CLI (5.8k stars, actively maintained Rust tool) into herdr as an fzf picker for interactively switching/creating/removing git worktrees, with worktrunk's lifecycle hooks (deps install, .env copy) running on create/teardown. But the core UX — pick-a-branch-in-fzf — doesn't match this user's actual worktree pattern: he's a coordinator that programmatically creates one worktree per spawned worker via git/EnterWorktree, sets pane cwd at creation, and does pre-flight setup centrally in his own scripts (per his agent-coordination rules) — there's no human sitting at an fzf prompt picking branches. If the hook system itself is wanted, `wt` is directly scriptable from his own coordinator code without this herdr glue at all.
deep_dives.gotchas: Platforms macOS+Linux (fine on CachyOS). Deps are lightweight (fzf, jq, bash) but it hard-requires installing `worktrunk` (`wt`) as a separate binary/config surface. Open bug #3 ("Plugin always creates worktree from default branch") is a real functional problem for anyone branching worktrees off a feature branch rather than main — exactly the kind of workflow a per-worker-worktree setup implies. Open bug #5: removing a worktree switches focus to the wrong workspace. Plugin itself is ~200 lines of bash (auditable in minutes) plus a `herdr-plugin.toml` manifest — no build step, no compiled runtime beyond what's already on the system.
deep_dives.maintenance: herdr-worktrunk repo: created 2026-06-28, last push 2026-07-03 (~2 weeks old), 30 stars, 2 open issues (both real functional bugs, unfixed). Upstream `worktrunk` itself is far more mature/active: created Oct 2025, 5,825 stars, pushed today (2026-07-14), 36 open issues but clearly a live, popular project — the risk sits almost entirely in the thin, young glue layer, not the underlying tool.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No feature overlap with herdr-agent-team's orchestration surface — this plugin has zero event-hook/status-push/run-board/mesh-topology code, it's purely an interactive human worktree switcher. Worth a skim purely as a herdr-plugin-authoring reference: (1) the `[[actions]]`→`herdr plugin pane open --plugin ID --entrypoint X --placement split --direction down --cwd Y --focus` pattern for spawning a pane from an action — directly reusable for pushing a run-board/status pane from herdr-agent-team; (2) `$HERDR_PLUGIN_CONTEXT_JSON` (workspace_cwd/focused_pane_cwd) plus the full env-var inventory available inside plugin panes (HERDR_BIN_PATH, HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT, HERDR_PLUGIN_ID, HERDR_WORKSPACE_ID, HERDR_PANE_ID) — useful reference for what context his plugin's panes can read without re-deriving it; (3) a real gotcha worth stealing: resolving `source_workspace_id` via `herdr worktree list --cwd $PWD --json` before calling `herdr worktree open`, because calling it from inside an already-open worktree workspace gets rejected with `linked_worktree_source` — will bite herdr-agent-team if it opens per-worker worktree workspaces from within a worktree itself; (4) the orphaned-pane cleanup pattern (grep `pane list` by cwd prefix, close matches) is a clean model for star/mesh teardown when a worker's worktree is removed. None of this is a reason to install the plugin — just skim the two ~80-line scripts (`picker.sh`, `remove.sh`) once for these idioms.

deep_dives.name: collie (AltanS/collie)
deep_dives.value: Mobile PWA that shows which of your herdr agents is blocked/done and lets you reply from your phone keyboard — directly matches your stated "mobile notifications + remote approval" want, and is well-built (systemd --user service, atomic UI rebuilds, real security posture). But its payoff is situational (only fires when you're away from the desk, which for a desk-bound coordinator workflow isn't weekly-guaranteed) and it's gated behind a real one-time setup cost: Tailscale isn't in your environment notes/memory, so you'd be standing up a tailnet, MagicDNS, and locking down COLLIE_TRUSTED_USER/COLLIE_PUBLIC_HOSTS before first real use. Revisit and flip to install-now the moment you have Tailscale for any other reason, or find yourself repeatedly missing a blocked agent while out.
deep_dives.gotchas: Linux+macOS only (manifest declares both) — fine for CachyOS. Hard dep: Bun (you already have 1.3). Soft deps: Node.js (only for MagicDNS extraction, falls back fine) and systemd --user (you have it; falls back to nohup+pidfile otherwise). Runs single-user/tailnet-only by design — ships with two loud startup WARNINGs (COLLIE_TRUSTED_USER, COLLIE_PUBLIC_HOSTS both empty = open-by-default) that you must close manually; never `tailscale funnel` it. Needs `loginctl enable-linger` to survive reboots on a headless box.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very actively maintained — pushed today (2026-07-14), commits daily this week (v0.10.2→0.10.3 in the last 3 days fixing real composer/Enter-reliability bugs), 0 open issues, not archived, versioned+changelogged with hook-enforced conventions. Low risk of abandonment.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No product overlap with herdr-agent-team — Collie is a monitor/reply UI, not an orchestrator (no spawn, no AGENTS.md generation, no worktrees, no topology). But it's a goldmine of reusable Herdr-integration knowledge worth reading NOW regardless of the install verdict: (1) its HERDR_API.md is an empirically-verified, up-to-date map of the socket protocol (methods, event catalog, `pane.send_keys` key grammar — note it's NOT tmux syntax, `ctrl+c` not `C-c`, no PageUp/Home/Delete) that directly answers herdr-agent-team's own open TODO in herdr-plugin.toml confirming `pane.agent_status_changed` is real and pane_id-scoped. (2) Its "event-poked polling" architecture — `events.subscribe` only accelerates a `session.snapshot` poll cadence and is never trusted as sole source of truth ("a missed event costs one interval, never correctness") — is a direct challenge to herdr-agent-team's "god never polls" pure-push design; worth deciding deliberately whether push-only is safe or needs a periodic reconciliation snapshot as a safety net, since Collie's authors apparently didn't trust events alone against the same backend. (3) Its recent commit history (composer "settle before Enter"/accumulation fixes) is prior art for exactly the double-Enter/pane.send_keys reliability quirks herdr-agent-team's README already flags for Codex — worth diffing their fix against your wake-up pane-injection code before you hit the same bug. (4) Its "thin plugin manifest launches a systemd --user service that outlives Herdr restarts" pattern (ARCHITECTURE.md §3) is a candidate model if a future run-board dashboard for herdr-agent-team needs to persist across Herdr restarts.

deep_dives.name: herdr-mirror (nikok6)
deep_dives.value: Mirrors a REMOTE herdr server's workspaces/agents into your LOCAL sidebar over ssh (daemon reconciles remote state, one pane process per stream, watch-or-drive per host) — but the user's profile shows one machine (CachyOS/KDE), no second always-on box to mirror from, so the core feature has nothing to attach to. Not a fit for driving Codex/Claude worker panes on the same machine, which is his actual daily pattern.
deep_dives.gotchas: Rust binary, prebuilt release fetched via `scripts/install.sh` for macos/linux x86_64/aarch64 (dev installs build from source via cargo — fine on CachyOS toolchain). Requires non-interactive ssh key auth to every remote host. Hard version lock: needs herdr on the **preview channel** (`herdr channel set preview`, build 2026-06-30+) on BOTH machines until "terminal session" streams reach stable — meaningful maintenance burden (tracking a moving preview API) even if a second machine existed. 2 of 3 open issues are the maintainer's own bug reports (mouse SGR packet leakage into remote shells), 1 is a Windows-support request (irrelevant, user has no Windows).
deep_dives.maintenance: Actively maintained: pushed 2026-07-12 (2 days before checkout), commits as recent as bug-hardening ("mouse: reuse daemon ssh master for cheap foreground polls") — this is a live, fast-iterating solo project, not abandoned. 21 stars, 3 open issues (all triaged/legit, none stale), not archived.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Genuine architectural precedent for herdr-agent-team even though the product itself doesn't fit: (1) control-plane/data-plane split — one `daemon` process reconciles remote workspace state and pushes agent status, separate from one `pane` process per streamed pane doing the actual data transfer, explicitly so "a busy pane can't contend with or drop another's stream" and so sidebar status stays live "regardless of what any stream is doing." That's the exact decoupling his run-board needs: status-push channel must not block on or depend on any single worker's raw output stream. (2) Event-hook + poll hybrid — `[[events]] on = "workspace.focused" command = [...]` in herdr-plugin.toml triggers cheap reconciliation on focus, backed by a slow poll_seconds=60 fallback for self-healing; worth copying directly for a star/mesh topology that needs to reconcile "desired agent set" vs "actual panes" without polling constantly. (3) Context-inheriting pane actions — `remote-new-workspace`/`remote-split-right` etc. inherit host+cwd from the invoking (focused) mirror pane, mirroring herdr's own native keybind semantics with an Option/Alt modifier; that's a clean pattern for "spawn a new team member pane preserving the invoking pane's git worktree/cwd context" rather than requiring the coordinator to pass an explicit path. (4) hosts.toml per-target config (enabled/prefix/always_control overrides per host) is a reasonable template for per-worker-role config if herdr-agent-team grows host- or role-specific settings. Net: don't install it, but read src/daemon.rs, src/mirror.rs, and the events/actions split in herdr-plugin.toml as a worked example before finalizing the run-board's own event-hook wiring.

deep_dives.name: herdr-ntfy-notify (zom-2018)
deep_dives.value: Would push a phone/desktop ntfy alert the instant a Codex or Claude worker pane flips to 'done' or 'blocked', tagged with an agent-type emoji and the exact workspace/tab/pane-id to reconnect to — directly hitting the "babysitting worker panes" pain his own workflow notes already flag. But it is strictly notify-only: it closes the "I need to know" half of his want, not the "let me act from my phone" half (no remote-approval loop), so he'd still have to physically get back to the machine to unblock an agent.
deep_dives.gotchas: Runtime dep is just Node (ESM `notify.mjs`, uses native `fetch` + `execSync('curl')`) — he has node 26, no npm install needed, genuinely zero-dependency. Code is honestly OS-neutral (no launchd/osascript/macOS paths) despite declaring platforms=[linux,macos,windows], so the Linux claim checks out. Needs a reachable ntfy endpoint: defaults assume a local server at 127.0.0.1:10081 (self-hosted, not yet confirmed present on his box) with fallback to an external URL — swap in public ntfy.sh for zero setup, at a privacy cost. Manifest pins `min_herdr_version = "0.7.0"`; worth a one-line check that his herdr build clears that. No ntfy click-action/reply wiring in the payload, so "remote approval" would require him to extend the ~230-line notify.mjs himself.
deep_dives.maintenance: Single commit total, created and pushed same day (2026-06-23), ~3 weeks untouched as of 2026-07-14; 0 open issues, 0 PRs, no CI workflow in the repo — the 5 stars sit on top of what looks like a weekend project with no evidence of sustained real-world use, not a track record.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Worth mining as a working reference for herdr-agent-team's event-hook plumbing even if he never installs it as a runtime dependency. Concrete things to lift: (1) the `herdr-plugin.toml` `[[events]] on = "pane.agent_status_changed"` block wired straight to a shell command receiving `HERDR_PLUGIN_EVENT_JSON` / `HERDR_PLUGIN_CONTEXT_JSON` — confirms real field names (`agent_status`, `display_agent`/`agent`, `workspace`, `tab`, `pane_id`, `custom_status`, `state_labels.error`/`task`) his own run-board/status-pusher can treat as ground truth for the event schema. (2) `herdr plugin config-dir <plugin-id>` as the sanctioned per-plugin config location, instead of inventing a dotfile path. (3) The local-fast-path/remote-fallback probe (800ms timeout against a local server before falling back, curl-first then fetch-fallback) is reusable if his star/mesh topology ever needs to push status off-box, though unnecessary for same-machine pane-to-pane IPC. (4) Defensive non-fatal wrapper (`run().catch(...) -> process.exit(0)`) so a broken notifier can never break Herdr's event pipeline — worth enforcing as a convention in whatever hook scripts his own plugin generates. Where he should differentiate: his stated scope (spawn team, AGENTS.md protocol, status events to god pane, run-board, star/mesh topology, remote approval) is strictly larger than this plugin's one-way notify — if he wants phone-side approval he should look at ntfy's own action-button/POST-back mechanism as the closing piece, since this repo already proves the notify transport works end-to-end over the same channel.

deep_dives.name: herdr-worktree-from-pr
deep_dives.value: Keybind → fzf overlay of open PRs on the current repo → herdr creates/opens a git worktree checked out to that branch (handles fork PRs via pull/N/head into a local pr-N branch). Real fit for the mechanics Caio already lives in (worktree-per-thing, gh CLI, herdr panes), but the specific job — pulling an EXTERNAL PR down into a fresh worktree — is thin for him: limux's own CLAUDE.md says "External PRs are not a triage surface," it's a personal fork, and his own agent-authored PRs already have a worktree from the spawn step, so there's nothing new to check out. Value shows up only if he starts reviewing collaborator/contributor PRs on limux or another repo, or wants a fast way to pull down someone else's PR to poke at locally — occasional, not weekly.
deep_dives.gotchas: Linux-supported (platforms = ["linux","macos"] in herdr-plugin.toml, min_herdr_version 0.7.0). Needs fzf (falls back to a plain numbered prompt without it — CachyOS: `pacman -S fzf` if not already present), authenticated `gh`, `git`, and Node (herdr invokes `node bin/*.js` directly, no build/compile step, no bun/cargo). Young repo: created 2026-07-03 (11 days old), 4 stars, 1 fork, 1 open issue ("use direnv for gh commands," unresolved), not archived. 10 tests across lib/, `npm test` = `node --test`. Config: `prLimit`, `forkBranchPrefix`, `placement` (right/left/top/down/overlay via herdr pane split+swap), `fzfLayout`.
deep_dives.maintenance: 8 commits over one week (2026-07-03 to 2026-07-11), then 3 days quiet as of today (2026-07-14) — active early-stage development, not stale, but too young to call it battle-tested; single unresolved minor issue.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Doesn't overlap herdr-agent-team's core job (heterogeneous team spawn, AGENTS.md protocol gen, event push to god pane, star/mesh topology, run-board) — this plugin is single-action, no event-hook subscription at all (no `[[hooks]]` in its herdr-plugin.toml, only `[[actions]]` and `[[panes]]`; a companion repo `tdi/herdr-worktree-setup` is the one that hooks `worktree.created`, worth a separate look if hook-on-worktree-creation is relevant). Three implementation patterns worth stealing for herdr-agent-team: (1) `lib/pane.js`'s placement abstraction — herdr only natively splits right/down, so left/top are synthesized as split-then-`herdr pane swap --direction`, and `overlay` is a full-screen zoom; steal this table verbatim for a run-board pane that needs flexible placement. (2) `lib/worktree.js`'s idempotent create-or-open: `git worktree list --porcelain` scan for an existing branch, `herdr worktree create|open --branch --focus --json` picked by whether it already exists — this is the exact create-vs-reuse decision his worktree-per-worker flow needs and currently may be doing ad hoc. (3) the documented gotcha in pane.js comments: pane commands are launched relative to the *plugin's* root dir, so passing the invoking repo's cwd must go through `--env FOO_CWD=...`, never `--cwd` (that breaks command-path resolution) — directly transferable if herdr-agent-team's god pane spawns worker panes and needs to hand them the caller's working repo.

deep_dives.name: DIodide/herdr-telemetry
deep_dives.value: Out of the box this gives him nothing actionable: it's a raw ndjson-to-webhook pipe (workspace/tab/pane/worktree lifecycle, agent status diffs, derived run start/stop/duration, optional focus-interval and per-run token/model counters), not a dashboard or a notifier — to get any visible value he'd have to stand up and maintain his own receiving endpoint plus a UI, which is real extra build work, not "install and benefit this week." Its own `notify.*` toasts only cover daemon health (socket lost, endpoint down, update available), not "agent needs your input" — so it does not deliver the desktop/mobile-notification or remote-approval workflow he actually wants.

deep_dives.name: herdr-push (dcolinmorgan)
deep_dives.value: herdr-push lets any herdr pane (Claude, Codex, or other agent) fire a curl POST the instant it goes "blocked," feeding a phone/Telegram approval loop — the exact "approve a stalled worker without opening the laptop" gap in his current file-brief-and-poll workflow. But the plugin itself is inert: real payoff only starts once he's separately cloned and is running dcolinmorgan/herdr-remote's relay, so this is a two-repo commitment, not a one-command install.
deep_dives.gotchas: herdr-push itself is zero-dependency (curl + python3-or-jq, both already present) and its herdr-plugin.toml explicitly declares `platforms = ["macos", "linux"]`, so it does run on CachyOS — no macOS gate on the plugin proper. The catch is everything downstream: it's a pure fire-and-forget notifier with no standalone value. Real use requires cloning herdr-remote and running `relay/start.sh`, which needs `uv` (PEP-723 inline deps: websockets, zeroconf, auto-installed) and, for a public phone URL, `cloudflared` (or LAN-only via its zeroconf discovery — no tunnel needed if phone is on the same network). herdr-remote's advertised "zero config" 10-second install is the Herdi.app macOS menu-bar client (Swift, .dmg) — that path is closed to him. And the richer interactions (`/trust`, `/interrupt`, `/reply` — actual one-tap *approval*, not just viewing) live in the Telegram bot (`uv run relay/herdr_telegram.py`), which needs a bot token + chat ID set up separately; the phone web app (herdr-demo.pages.dev) is more of a live-viewer. So "install herdr-push" in practice means: install plugin (trivial) + stand up a Python relay service + choose tunnel-vs-LAN + configure a Telegram bot, before any approval-from-phone actually works.

deep_dives.name: herdr-resurrect (ntindle)
deep_dives.value: Closes a real gap in your daily coordinator workflow: today, a herdr crash/restart brings your worktree panes back as bare shells, losing every live Codex/Claude worker. This plugin replays the actual agent commands with native resume flags (claude --resume/--continue, codex resume — recovered from each CLI's own on-disk session store keyed by pane cwd) plus any allowlisted dev servers, so "restore last snapshot" turns a lost fleet back into a running one. Event-driven autosave (debounced 20s) is zero-maintenance once installed; auto-restore-on-boot is opt-in so it won't surprise you.
deep_dives.gotchas: Requires herdr >= 0.7.0 (verify your version) and Node >=16 — trivially satisfied (you have node 26) and it has zero npm deps, so nothing to build. Manifest declares linux/macos/windows, no macOS-only code paths — confirmed Linux-safe (ps-based process-tree capture, tested against real GNU ps output per commit log). Restore is idempotent (only fills idle panes) and dry-runnable, so downside risk of trying it is low. Agent-resume fidelity depends on each CLI's own --resume actually restoring context — outside this plugin's control. It's 8 days old with 3 stars and a single contributor, so treat it as young: skim bin/restore.js before trusting --recreate on a complex nested-grid layout (README admits deep grids are only approximated), and note it proposes an upstream herdr server.ready event that doesn't exist yet, so its boot-detection is a workaround that could need updating if herdr's plugin API changes.

But three implementation patterns are directly reusable for your run-board persistence: (1) lib/agent-sessions.js recovers each agent's session id from its own CLI's on-disk store keyed by pane cwd (claude: ~/.claude/projects/, codex: ~/.codex/sessions/, plus copilot/cursor) — steal this instead of reinventing team-member session tracking across restarts; (2) herdr-plugin.toml's event-hook set (workspace.created/closed, pane.created/closed/exited, pane.agent_detected) is a working reference manifest for wiring your own "push status events to god pane"; (3) the boot-token claim-once pattern (detect a fresh server boot via socket-file rewrite, let the first event handler claim it before autosave overwrites pre-crash state) solves the exact "run once on start, no server.ready event yet" problem your run-board will hit. deep_dives.overlap_detail:
Direct overlap with herdr-agent-team's planned run-board/status-push feature — same job (working/blocked fleet status, deduplicated per agent, across workspaces). Two things worth studying, one thing worth doing differently:
(1) STUDY the manifest shape: `herdr-plugin.toml` registers a `[[panes]]` (placement="split", a plain subprocess command) plus `[[actions]]` that call `herdr plugin pane open --entrypoint <id> --direction down|right` — a clean, minimal reference for how a herdr plugin declares a TUI pane + keybinding-invokable actions, useful if herdr-agent-team's own plugin manifest isn't already this explicit.
(2) STUDY the data model: `AgentState`/`AgentSnapshot`/`StateTransition` in a `common` crate, dedup-by-pane_id (only the latest task per agent survives), JSONL append with a 7-day rolling cleanup — a workable, low-ceremony persistence pattern for a run-board's history if herdr-agent-team doesn't have one yet.
(3) DO DIFFERENTLY: herdr-insight is poll-based — it spawns a fresh `herdr` subprocess (`Command::new`) on a 2-second timer to re-fetch and re-diff full pane state, plus a separate subprocess per pane for output preview. That's the opposite of the user's own design goal (push status events to the god pane) and is the weaker architecture for token/resource efficiency at fleet scale — N subprocess spawns every 2s scales linearly with pane count. herdr-agent-team's event-driven push should out-perform this by construction; that's the concrete differentiator to lean on rather than copy. Also worth noting as a gap to fill, not copy: herdr-insight has no push/mobile/remote-approval surface at all — it's purely a pane you must have open to see; if herdr-agent-team wants to beat it on the user's actual priority list, the notification/remote-approval piece (not the timeline visual itself) is where the real differentiation value is.

deep_dives.name: herdr-fresh-worktree
deep_dives.value: Every worker worktree Caio spins up for a Codex/Claude agent starts from whatever local `main`/`kde-port` happened to be checked out at creation time — this plugin silently resets a brand-new, untouched worktree onto origin's live default branch (via `fetch origin HEAD` + `reset --hard FETCH_HEAD`, name-agnostic) the instant herdr creates it, so per-ticket worktrees never carry stale-base merge pain later. It's a pure background safety net (no daily interaction, zero config), which fits his "coordinator does the judgment, automation does the plumbing" style exactly.
deep_dives.gotchas: Requires herdr 0.7.0+ (Caio has 0.7.3 — satisfied) and `node`+`git` on PATH (both present, node 26). Pure JS, zero npm deps, single 94-line file — trivially auditable before trusting an auto-reset hook. Platforms list explicitly includes linux (works on CachyOS/KDE, not macOS-only). One caveat specific to his setup: this only fires on herdr's own `worktree.created` event — his coordinator/pane-worker pattern sometimes creates worktrees via `git worktree add` directly from Claude Code's EnterWorktree tool or scripted pre-flight rather than through herdr's UI/API; verify the event actually fires for however he provisions worktrees (herdr CLI `worktree create` vs raw git) before assuming full coverage. Also remember the project's own `ghostty` vendored-submodule symlink trap (memory: worktree-ghostty-symlink-trap) — a `reset --hard` on a fresh worktree wouldn't touch that (submodule content untracked by this plugin's guards), so the existing `git update-index --skip-worktree` workaround is still needed separately.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very young (created 2026-06-25, last push same day, only 2 commits) and tiny adoption (2 stars, 0 open issues) — essentially unmaintained-because-finished rather than abandoned: it's a narrow, complete, well-specced single-purpose script (has a full spec doc with worked cases + rejected-alternatives log, plus a `node --test` suite covering guards, idempotency, concurrency, and the offline path). Low risk from staleness since the surface area is small enough to fully read in five minutes, but there's no track record of the author fixing bugs under real-world load — worth a quick self-test (`node --test`) before trusting it on a repo with real work.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No functional overlap with herdr-agent-team (that's heterogeneous team orchestration/status/topology; this is a single worktree-hygiene hook) — but it's a strong pattern reference for the worktree-fan-out piece of Caio's own plugin. Three things worth stealing: (1) The `herdr-plugin.toml` `[[events]] on = "worktree.created" command = [...]` registration shape is the exact mechanism his agent-team plugin needs anyway to auto-prep each worker's worktree (freshen base, maybe also symlink shared build deps like `ghostty/`) instead of doing it via a manual coordinator pre-flight step, as his agent-coordination.md rules currently prescribe by hand. (2) The "is this worktree pristine enough to safely auto-mutate" guard chain (no upstream tracking, no matching remote branch, clean tree, re-checked immediately before the destructive op, and — the clever one — commit-reachable-from-another-local-ref rather than fast-forward-only, so a worktree cut from a feature branch still gets freshened) is a directly reusable template for any auto-action his agent-team plugin takes on a freshly spawned worker's worktree before the worker starts writing. (3) `fetch origin HEAD` + `reset --hard FETCH_HEAD` as the name-agnostic way to grab "whatever the remote's default branch currently is" is cleaner than resolving `origin/main` vs `origin/master` by string, and dodges stale local `origin/HEAD` and concurrent-fetch ref contention — useful if herdr-agent-team's worktree fan-out ever needs to independently confirm each worker branch is current before it starts. Net: install it for the direct benefit, and read `specs/worktree-fresh.md` when building agent-team's own worktree-created hook — it's a better-documented version of the same guard logic he'd otherwise write from scratch.</overlap_detail>

deep_dives.name: herdr-workboard
deep_dives.value: A kanban TUI where board=workspace, column=tab, task-session=pane, with live agent-state-driven auto-sync — directly useful as a visual run-board for the multi-agent fleets this user already runs, but the tool is one day old (v0.1 to v0.2.1 in 24h, single author, 0 stars) and its own PLAN.md admits the orchestration is unsafely coupled to the TUI render loop (sync dies when the board pane closes; concurrent TUI instances race-write the same JSON with no lock) — exactly the failure mode that would bite this user's heavy, always-multiple-panes-open workflow.
deep_dives.gotchas: Needs bun on the PATH herdr was started with and herdr >=0.7.1 — both already satisfied here (bun 1.3.14, herdr 0.7.3), so no install friction. platforms=["macos","linux"] explicit in herdr-plugin.toml, so Linux is fully supported, not an afterthought. Real caveat is architectural, not platform: orchestration only runs while a board TUI pane is open, and multiple open board views (pinned + overlay) can silently drop each other's writes to the shared board JSON (last-writer-wins, no lock) — the author's own next milestone is fixing this via a headless "guardian" pane.
deep_dives.maintenance: Repo created 2026-07-13, last push 2026-07-14 (today) — one-day-old, 4 commits, v0.1->v0.2.1 same day, single author, 0 stars, 0 open issues (too new to signal either way); very hot but zero track record.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Closest known prior art to herdr-agent-team's run-board feature. Steal: (1) the 1:1 physical mapping of task-state to pane-location (moving a card = moving a pane between tabs via pane.move) as a single-source-of-truth state model instead of a separate synced status field; (2) the PATH-probing multi-agent-CLI registry (13 known agents: claude/codex/hermes/opencode/copilot/cursor-agent/droid/kimi/kilo/qoder/gemini/pi/omp) with graceful unknown-status degradation for unrecognized agents — directly applicable to heterogeneous agent-team orchestration; (3) its idle-pane-reuse + even-tiling algorithm for populating a tab with new session arrivals. Do differently: their own architecture doc concludes orchestration must NOT live inside a rendering TUI process (sync stops when the view closes; concurrent instances race-write shared JSON) — their fix-in-progress is a headless 'guardian' pane (visible, workspace-scoped, restartable) as single writer with TUIs submitting intents rather than writing directly. Build herdr-agent-team's status-push-to-god-pane and run-board persistence with that separation from day one rather than retrofitting it later. Also worth a deliberate decision, not drift: their implicit natural-language 'say BLOCKED and why' prompt convention + string-match detection vs. herdr-agent-team's more explicit AGENTS.md-generated protocol.

deep_dives.name: herdr-kanban (KokiKono/herdr-kanban)
deep_dives.value: A ratatui TUI Kanban board (TODO/IN PROGRESS/DONE) that auto-creates a linked herdr tab when a task moves to IN PROGRESS and lets you jump to/close that tab from the board. It's a manual, single-user task tracker with no agent-status awareness, no notifications, no worktree or gh integration — it duplicates ground his file-based task_plan.md/progress.md/findings.md system already covers, and adds no capability toward the things he actually tracks daily (fleet status, blocked-agent approval, worktree state). Not worth installing as-is; his own herdr-agent-team run-board will supersede it in scope.

Tab auto-create/focus/close only works with HERDR_ENV=1; outside herdr it degrades to a standalone board with no tab linkage. rusqlite uses the `bundled` feature so no system sqlite dependency to worry about.

(2) The idempotent "stock-take" import (`i` key: scan all workspace tabs, create TODO cards only for tabs not yet tracked, dedup by tab_id) is a good pattern for reconciling a persisted board against live pane state without duplicate entries — relevant if his run-board needs to resync after a crash or manual pane changes. Differentiate, don't imitate: this plugin is poll-based (press `r` to reload from SQLite) with a thin synchronous CLI-wrapper (shell out to `herdr tab create/focus/close/list`, parse JSON, no event stream) — his stated design of pushing status events to a god pane is a materially stronger model than this poll/reload approach, so there's no reason to downgrade to its pattern, only to note it as the low bar being exceeded.

deep_dives.value: Ratatui TUI + CLI that runs declarative TOML "workflows" with a fuzzy-pick + field-form UI; its bundled launch-agent example replaces two hand-typed `herdr worktree create` + `herdr agent start` commands with a form (pick agent from roster, pick base branch, type a prompt). deep_dives.gotchas: Pure-Rust static binary, builds via `cargo`/build.sh on install (rust already present here, zero extra toolchain cost); runs natively on Linux (platforms=[macos,linux]), no macOS-only blocker. Self-labeled "Beta (v0.2.x)" and the TOML workflow format already replaced an earlier .mjs format once, so schema may still shift. Needs herdr >=0.7.0; the launch-agent example additionally shells out to jq and assumes a git repo.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very young (created 2026-06-29) but actively pushed: 3 tagged releases (v0.1.0->v0.2.0->v0.3.0) in under 2 weeks, last push 2026-07-10 (4 days before today); 0 stars/forks/open issues signals near-zero external adoption or scrutiny yet — solo-author project, not herdr core team.

Three patterns worth stealing for his own plugin's spawn-agent-variant picker: (1) TOML-declarative workflow + ratatui field form (text/multiline/fuzzy-select via `choices_command`) as the UI layer for picking an agent variant; (2) single source-of-truth roster file parsed by one shared script (`agents.sh list`/`get`) so the picker's shown choices and the launcher's resolved command can never drift; (3) a deliberate single-responsibility seam — it launches the agent in its own new pane and explicitly leaves the worktree's root pane untouched so a separate `worktree.created` hook can do per-worktree setup without racing the agent for that pane. That last pattern is a concrete argument for splitting herdr-agent-team's create-worktree / run-setup / spawn-agent(s) into independently hookable steps instead of one monolithic call. Treat it as a component to borrow from, not a competing product — it is intentionally not team-aware.

deep_dives.name: herdr-agent-dashboard
deep_dives.value: A live bun/TSX TUI table over `herdr agent list` (status/agent/task/cwd/tab/pane, arrow-nav, enter-to-jump-to-pane) toggled by a single keybind — directly matches his "monitoring agent fleets" habit and costs almost nothing to try (bun/python3 already on this machine, no daemon, no protocol to conflict with his own plugin). Status/cwd/tab/pane/focus-jump still work for every agent regardless (degrades gracefully, doesn't error).

Also note the rendering substrate choice — @opentui/react (diff-rendered React-for-terminal) — as a candidate for the run-board's own UI instead of hand-rolled ANSI, worth a quick eval.
