deep_dives.name: herdr-worktree-lifecycle
deep_dives.value: Auto-runs a repo-committed `scripts/worktree-setup <branch> <worktree> <primary>` on worktree create/open and `scripts/worktree-teardown` on remove, with status pushed to the pane and a completion toast — could kill his known recurring "ghostty symlink + skip-worktree" gotcha in limux automatically instead of by hand each time, and could auto-clean worker worktrees for herdr-agent-team. But value only materializes per repo once he writes and commits that wrapper script — it's not a drop-in fleet-wide win given he juggles many varied projects.
deep_dives.gotchas: Pure Rust (serde/serde_json/fs2 only), builds with plain `cargo build --release` — no node/bun/docker, trivial on his toolchain. `platforms = ["linux","macos"]` in herdr-plugin.toml — Linux explicitly supported. Real cost is the integration ABI, not the runtime: every repo that wants this must commit executable `scripts/worktree-setup`/`scripts/worktree-teardown` taking positional `<branch> <worktree> <primary>` args (cwd is the plugin's install dir, NOT the worktree — scripts can't rely on $PWD). That's per-repo authoring/maintenance burden across his many projects, which is exactly what the triage flagged as more invasive than the config-driven provisioner plugin for the same job.
deep_dives.maintenance: All activity in a single day (2026-06-29): init + 2 reviewed PRs (0.1.0→0.1.1), then silent for ~2 weeks through 2026-07-14. 0 stars, 0 open issues — looks like a finished, narrow-scope first-party (qdentity org, likely herdr-author-adjacent) utility rather than an actively-iterated community plugin. Code quality is solid for its scope: has a real test suite (tests/cli.rs), handles malformed events by exiting 0 rather than crashing, and an explicit review-driven commit ("exit-0 on recoverable errors, best-effort state") shows it was hardened once, not abandoned mid-build.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Strong pattern-reuse value for herdr-agent-team's status-push/run-board design, even if the plugin itself isn't installed. Concrete primitives worth copying: (1) `herdr pane report-metadata <pane_id> --source <name> --custom-status <text>` / `--clear-custom-status` — the exact CLI surface for pushing an ephemeral status string onto a pane's UI, i.e. the "push status events to god pane" mechanism his own plugin needs; no need to invent a new channel. (2) `herdr notification show <title> --body <b> --position top-right --sound <done|request>` — a ready-made completion/failure toast primitive that maps directly onto his desktop-notification requirement. (3) `herdr pane list --workspace <id>` returning JSON with pane_id/tab_id, used to resolve which pane to attach status to from workspace_id+active_tab_id in the event payload — a clean pattern for star/mesh topology status routing. (4) Cross-process serialization via `fs2::FileExt` advisory locks keyed on a stable hash of the lexically-normalized worktree path (deliberately NOT the branch, since branch may be empty) — directly solves the "multiple workers touching overlapping worktree state concurrently" problem his agent-team plugin will hit. (5) Atomic write-then-rename for status marker files plus bounded log retention (keep latest 20 per worktree) under `HERDR_PLUGIN_STATE_DIR` — a worked example of the on-disk run-board state he's planning to build himself. (6) Discipline worth copying: every herdr CLI call is timeout-bounded (2s) and best-effort (`let _ = ...`) so a notification/status-push failure never fails the underlying operation. Net: read `src/lib.rs` (functions `resolve_herdr_pane`, `report_setup_status`, `acquire_path_lock`, `write_setup_status`) as a reference before designing herdr-agent-team's own status/lock layer, rather than reinventing those primitives from scratch.

deep_dives.name: gren-herdr (langtind/gren-herdr)
deep_dives.value: Automates exactly the manual step his own coordination rules make him do by hand — worktree fan-out pre-flight (env symlinks, deps install, toolchain bootstrap) — by running it in the new worktree's own pane with a real TTY the instant herdr fires `worktree.created`. But getting that value means migrating his worktree creation off plain `git worktree add`/herdr's native worktree commands onto `gren` (a separate Go TUI worktree manager with its own hook-config DSL, `.gren/` per-project config, and `gren delete` for teardown) — a workflow change, not a drop-in.
deep_dives.gotchas: Runtime deps: `gren` CLI ≥0.11.0 (0.16.0 for `--interactive` TTY hooks) on PATH + `fzf` + `jq` + bash. gren is Go (`go install github.com/langtind/gren@latest` — trivial since go 1.26.5 is already on this machine; also Homebrew/prebuilt-binary, no cargo/node needed). Platforms explicitly macos+linux — fine for CachyOS. No sandboxing/systemd concerns. Real friction is conceptual, not technical: adopting it means running worktrees through gren's model (its own `worktree_dir` convention, `gren init` hook config, `gren delete` for cleanup — herdr's native sidebar-delete silently skips gren's teardown hooks, an easy footgun) instead of the plain git-worktree flow his rules currently assume.
deep_dives.maintenance: Very early but genuinely live: repo created 2026-07-03 (11 days old), last push 2026-07-12, 0 open issues, not archived, single maintainer (langtind, who also authors gren itself — Go, 0 stars, 13 open issues, pushed same day). Commit history shows real incident-driven fixes dated within days of each other (a 2026-07-12 stdout-parsing bug found and patched same day; two prior fixes chasing herdr's own upstream API changes on 07-07 and 07-09) — the author is actively dogfooding it, but the plugin has already broken against herdr's own churn twice in under two weeks, signaling ongoing coupling risk to keep up with herdr releases.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Directly relevant primitive for herdr-agent-team, worth studying rather than depending on. Two reusable patterns: (1) subscribing to herdr's `worktree.created` event in `herdr-plugin.toml` (`[[events]] on = "worktree.created"`) to run setup automatically the moment a worktree is born — this is the clean, race-free answer to the exact cwd-split-brain problem his own memory already flags for codex panes (pane cwd must be set at creation, not via a prompt-level `cd`); herdr's event fires with the new checkout's path so the hook pane inherits the right cwd by construction, no `--cwd` guessing needed. (2) The `[[panes]]` manifest pattern (`gren hook-run --interactive` running AS the pane's process to get a real TTY for interactive setup like 1Password unlock) is a cleaner primitive than his current file-based brief/report loop for anything that needs live interactive output during worktree bootstrap. Scope differs though — gren-herdr is single-purpose (env/deps bootstrap on worktree creation), herdr-agent-team is heterogeneous team orchestration (AGENTS.md protocol, status-push to god pane, star/mesh topology, run-board) — not a competing plugin, no functional overlap beyond the shared event-hook+pane-injection substrate. Also worth a skim: `docs/debugging.md` in the repo documents a failure-signature table (polluted stdout breaking JSON parsing, discarded stderr masking root cause, registration-vs-setup race) — a preview of the exact fragility classes his own event handlers will hit when parsing output from spawned CLI tools, useful as a pre-mortem before he designs his own.

Its dependency `gwm-cli` (same author) is healthier: 23 stars, CI badge, v1.0.3 released 2026-07-09, pushed as recently as 2026-07-13 (day before today) — the underlying tool is real and active even though this thin wrapper is unproven.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Several patterns worth stealing verbatim for herdr-agent-team, even skipping the plugin itself: (1) Adopt-not-own via a single guarded chokepoint — `lib/common.sh::adopt_worktree` is the ONLY path that calls into herdr (`worktree open --path` / `workspace close`), and a grep-assert test fails the build if any script reaches around it to call `worktree create`/`--branch` directly. That's a clean template for keeping herdr-agent-team's worktree-per-agent reflection separate from whatever actually owns worktree lifecycle (his own git glue). (2) Event double-fire avoidance — distinguishes `worktree.opened` (fired by its own adopt calls) from `worktree.created` (native, outside-plugin) so its `on_worktree_created.sh` bootstrap hook never double-runs on its own work; directly applicable to his run-board's status-event plumbing. (3) `gwm.exec` — fan a command across every worktree with a checkmark/x rollup — is close to 'exec-across-worktrees' he'll want for the run-board/fleet-monitoring mechanic. (4) Pane-cwd-from-context-JSON — every action reads `workspace_cwd // focused_pane_cwd` out of `$HERDR_PLUGIN_CONTEXT_JSON` and passes it explicitly as `--cwd` to `plugin pane open`; this is the exact fix for the pane-cwd split-brain gotcha already in his CLAUDE.local.md (workers inherit launch cwd; prompt-level cd doesn't chdir) — confirms `--cwd` at pane-open time is the correct, herdr-native way to do it. (5) Link-handler URL validation — the GitHub PR link handler anchors the regex and re-validates in `pr_number_from_url`, extracting only the PR number before it ever reaches a shell command, never passing a raw/forged URL downstream — a good template if herdr-agent-team ever wires clickable links (issue/PR URLs in agent output) to actions. Net: don't install, but skim `lib/common.sh` and `herdr-plugin.toml` once as reference before designing herdr-agent-team's own worktree-adoption and event-hook code.

deep_dives.name: ditwrd/herdr-remote-worktrunk
deep_dives.value: Lets you turn a picked GitHub issue/branch/PR into a worktree + herdr workspace via one fzf flow instead of typing gh/git/wt/herdr by hand — real but narrow, since it's a one-at-a-time human picker, not something his scripted multi-worker god-agent fan-out would call.
deep_dives.gotchas: Linux supported (herdr-plugin.toml platforms=[linux,macos]). All stated deps already satisfied on this box (herdr 0.7.3 ≥0.7.0, gh 2.96.0 exactly meets min, fzf 0.74.0 ≥0.56.2, jq 1.8.2 ≥1.7.1) EXCEPT worktrunk's `wt` binary, which is not installed — needs `cargo install worktrunk` first (cargo present, low cost). No config burden beyond an optional keybinding in config.toml.
deep_dives.maintenance: 4 days old, 2 commits total (both same day, 2026-07-10), 0 stars, 0 open issues — no track record yet; upstream fork base (devashish2203/herdr-worktrunk, 30 stars) is more established and was updated 2 days ago. The wrapped dependency, max-sixty/worktrunk, is the opposite: 5,825 stars, pushed today, clearly thriving.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: No orchestration overlap — this is a one-shot interactive fzf picker invoked from a workspace context menu, not a background service; it never touches agent status, star/mesh topology, or event push. The one thing worth lifting for herdr-agent-team: helpers.sh's create_herdr_workspace() idiom — `herdr worktree list --cwd "$PWD" --json` to resolve the parent root_workspace_id, then `herdr worktree open --workspace <id> --path <wtpath> --label <label> --focus --json` with a non-fatal warning on failure. That's a clean, already-solved pattern for registering a freshly created per-worker worktree as a herdr workspace, directly reusable instead of reinventing the JSON contract.

deep_dives.gotchas: Node.js + npm only (light — no heavy toolchain, aligns with user's toolchain already having node 26 installed). herdr-plugin.toml explicitly declares platforms=["linux"] — confirmed Linux-native, no macOS-isms. Needs its own Telegram bot (BotFather token) configured in ~/.config/herdr-telegram/config.toml, plus a manually-launched or event-bootstrapped daemon (`node dist/index.js --daemon`, pidfile-guarded, auto-relaunched via the `pane.agent_status_changed` hook so it self-heals if killed). Response detection does NOT trust herdr's own `agent_status` field — the author's code comment says it can be inaccurate and instead debounces on pane-content stability (no change for 3s) anchored on the last line of sent input; worth knowing independent of installing this plugin, since the user's own god-agent workflow leans on `agent wait --status`. 1-day-old repo (created 2026-07-13, still receiving same-day bugfixes from a real external reporter) — expect rough edges.
deep_dives.maintenance: Brand new: created 2026-07-13, last push 2026-07-14 (today) with an active same-day bugfix (daemon launch-dir robustness + herdr-bin PATH resolution) driven by real external user feedback ("Laszlo"), 0 open issues, not archived, MIT/TypeScript, has a test suite (vitest) and vitepress docs site — good hygiene for its age, but only ~1 day of track record and 0 stars/no adoption signal yet.
deep_dives.name: mvallebr/herdr-telegram-plugin
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Directly in the herdr-agent-team problem space (event-hooks + pane control + status tracking) and worth mining for patterns even if not installed: (1) `herdr-plugin.toml` registers a `[[events]] on = "pane.agent_status_changed"` hook whose command just checks a pidfile and spawns a detached companion daemon if not running — a clean, minimal 'self-healing bootstrap' pattern for pushing status events to a god pane without keeping a service always-on. (2) `herdr-client.ts`'s `herdrBin()` resolves the CLI via env override → `which` → common install paths (`/usr/local/bin`, `~/.local/bin`, `~/.cargo/bin`) → PATH fallback, with descriptive spawnSync error wrapping (errno + binary path) — directly reusable for herdr-agent-team's own CLI-shelling code. (3) `watcher.ts` polls `herdr tab list`/`agent list` and reconciles a known-state map against current panes (added/removed/renamed), including a periodic health-check that detects externally-deleted state and recreates it — this is the same reconciliation shape a star/mesh run-board needs for its live topology view. (4) `wait-loop.ts`'s `extractResponseSince`/stability-window approach explicitly avoids trusting herdr's `agent_status` field (noted as 'sometimes inaccurate') and instead anchors on the last input line + debounces on content-stability — a caution and a technique worth adopting for herdr-agent-team's own status-push/run-board completion detection. Net: skip installing it for its stated purpose (Telegram bridge, no blocked-agent enrichment) but read its ~6 short source files as a reference implementation before building the equivalent event-hook/status-push plumbing in herdr-agent-team.
deep_dives.value: Low direct install value right now: it does exactly one thing (map each herdr pane to a Telegram forum topic, forward text both ways, zero LLM) with no blocked-agent-specific enrichment, so for the user's stated priority — mobile notifications + remote approval of blocked agents — the already-identified higher-fit Telegram/Slack bridge plugin covers the same niche better; running two bots/daemons for the same job is redundant.

deep_dives.name: herdr-matter-wall
deep_dives.value: A glanceable, manually-refreshed status wall over subdirectories with context.md/README.md markers — nice for a portfolio/monorepo of many small projects, but it's a passive doc-summarizer, not a live monitor of his running Codex/Claude worker fleet, so it doesn't cover his actual daily need (blocked-agent alerts, worktree/PR flow, live status). Would also need him to start maintaining per-subdir context.md files, which his current repo-root-centric docs (HANDOFF.md/task_plan.md) don't follow.
deep_dives.gotchas: Needs claude CLI (already has, logged in), jq, herdr >=0.7.0 (verify his herdr version), bash >=3.2 — all present/trivial on CachyOS. Pure bash, no build, `herdr plugin link` for local dev. Cards are static snapshots (only update via manual --refresh <slug>, one at a time via lockfile) — not push/live. Needs context.md or README.md per subdirectory to produce real cards; without it you get '(no summary)' fallback boxes. ~5 haiku calls per `open` (cheap) but still real token spend each time you open/refresh.
deep_dives.maintenance: Created 2026-07-06, last push 2026-07-14 (today) — 4 releases in 8 days, single committer, 0 open issues, 0 stars, not archived: fast-moving but only 8 days old and unproven.
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Not literal fleet monitoring (it's read-only doc summarization of static subdirectories, manually refreshed, no live worker tracking or notifications) — but three patterns are directly reusable for herdr-agent-team's run-board: (1) the fenced <<<CARD...CARD>>> structured schema (STATUS/PHASE/DEADLINE/RISK/RECENT/AWAITING/NEXT with bulleted sub-lists) as an LLM-emittable status contract; (2) the render/data split — a pure-bash, LLM-free renderer that never errors the whole board out and falls back gracefully on malformed input; (3) one-item-per-full-screen-tab layout via `herdr tab create --label` plus idempotent workspace teardown-and-recreate, and a per-item refresh lockfile to avoid races — all worth mirroring for a live agent-fleet board. Also worth copying: strict read-only --allowedTools scoping for any summarizer/status sub-agent, kept separate in permissions from the worker agent it describes.

deep_dives.name: Newt6611/herdr-plugin-rust
deep_dives.value: Not a tool to install/run — it's a Rust SDK (env/event parsing, CLI-JSON client, socket runtime) for building herdr plugins. Direct value: it may pre-answer two of your herdr-agent-team spec's own open build TODOs (manifest event name + JSON payload shape for pane.agent_status_changed), worth reading before you hand-implement the same plumbing.
deep_dives.gotchas: Pure Rust crate (tokio + serde + toml + interprocess), no system deps, builds fine on Linux/CachyOS. rust-version 1.75+. SocketRuntime (the long-running push-event mode closest to your ADR-0002 design) is explicitly marked "still in testing" in its own README — don't lean on it for reliability guarantees yet.
deep_dives.maintenance: Created 2026-07-07, 9 crates.io releases in 3 days, last push 2026-07-09 (5 days stale), 0 open issues, 0 stars, single maintainer (contributor to the real ogulcancelik/herdr, dogfoods this SDK in his own shipped herdr-tab-title plugin)
deep_dives.overlap_detail: Direct overlap with herdr-agent-team. Your Cargo.toml currently has zero deps with a comment planning to hand-roll HERDR_BIN_PATH CLI+JSON wrapping — this crate already did that (HerdrEnv::from_env(), HerdrClient, typed EventKind/EventEnvelope for all 19 herdr events, AgentClient/WorktreeClient/PluginClient wrapping agent start/read/send/wait, worktree create/open/remove, and plugin-authored panes). Concretely: its events.rs answers your spec §9 TODO — EventKind::PaneAgentStatusChanged.dot_name() = "pane.agent_status_changed" with a full typed payload struct (pane_id, workspace_id, agent_status, agent, title, display_agent, custom_status, state_labels) — cross-check this against `herdr api schema --json` rather than trust it outright, since Newt6611 hasn't shipped a plugin that itself subscribes to this event (his herdr-tab-title only uses tab/workspace events). Recommendation: don't add as a Cargo dependency (0-star, single-maintainer, 1-week-old, wrapping a closed-source schema) — instead read src/env.rs and src/events.rs as reference to verify/shortcut your own event-vocabulary and env-parsing work, consistent with the copy-don't-depend posture you already took in ADR-0005. Its PluginPaneOpenOptions/pane_open API is also a useful reference for your out-of-scope-for-v1 dashboard/run-board pane when you get there.

mediums.name: hotchpotch/herdr-tiny-fingers
mediums.summary: tmux-fingers-style overlay that hints and copies file paths, SHAs, UUIDs, IPs, URLs, and k8s names visible in the focused pane.
mediums.why: Real QoL for someone doing heavy terminal work with git SHAs and paths across many agent panes, but not core to his orchestration work.

mediums.name: kamaaina/herdr_sync
mediums.summary: Types a command into the focused pane and broadcasts it (pre-Enter) to all other panes in the current tab, tmux-synchronize-panes style; built in Zig.
mediums.why: Useful for running the same command across several worktree/build panes at once; rough/early (README admits install uncertainty), zig 0.16 build matches his toolchain.

mediums.name: tomasvarga/herdr-pickr
mediums.summary: Ctrl+click a GitHub PR/GitLab MR link in any pane and choose which reviewer tool (tuicr, hunk, plain diff, browser, or custom) opens it, config-driven per backend.
mediums.why: Nice router for gh-centric review workflows, but its value depends on already using tuicr/hunk, and it's mostly redundant with picking one tool by default.

mediums.name: blurname/herdr-git-tab-name
mediums.summary: Renames the active tab to the focused pane's git branch on focus/worktree events; falls back to detached:<sha>.
mediums.why: Small but genuinely useful given his worktree-per-worker pattern — keeps tabs legible without manual naming.

mediums.name: agustinvalencia/herdr-jump
mediums.summary: Two overlay pickers (bubbletea TUI) — one lists every agent grouped by space with a status dot (idle/working/blocked/done), the other lists workspaces — for jumping straight to one.
mediums.why: Genuinely useful for navigating a large agent fleet, but functionally overlaps with the simpler herdr-attention (cycle-to-next-urgent) so it's a nice-to-have rather than a must, not both are needed.

mediums.name: krystof018/herdr-git-status
mediums.summary: Background poller that prefixes sidebar space labels with CI status dots (GitHub Actions or GitLab pipelines, auto-detected) plus MR/PR number and review-state glyphs, and an on-demand detail pane.
mediums.why: Fits his gh-CLI-centric PR workflow well for at-a-glance CI status across worktrees, though it's about CI/PR state rather than agent status specifically.

mediums.name: tiny-send/tinysend-herdr
mediums.summary: Emails you (with a one-line summary) when an agent blocks/finishes/fails; reply to the email to answer the blocked agent or hand it its next task, via the tinysend mailbox service.
mediums.why: Directly implements 'remote approval of a blocked agent from your phone' which he explicitly wants, but it routes through a third-party mailbox service (tinysend.com) and its away-detection is macOS-only, adding cost/setup friction over a simpler notifier.

mediums.name: simoncrypta/agentic-dev-setup
mediums.summary: Installs a shareable herdr+worktrunk layout (sticky agent pane + review/explorer/terminal tabs) that auto-creates/closes herdr workspaces on worktree start/remove, cross-platform including Omarchy (Arch) and Ubuntu.
mediums.why: A declarative workspace layout with worktree-lifecycle hooks is close to what he wants, and it's Arch-compatible (Omarchy), but it hardcodes an opinionated stack (worktrunk, tuicr, nvim) he hasn't confirmed using.

mediums.name: scott306lr/herdr-plugin-hunk-autodiff
mediums.summary: Watches pane.agent_status_changed; when a coding agent goes idle with a dirty working tree, auto-opens a split running `hunk diff --watch` next to it.
mediums.why: Directly serves 'review of agent diffs' with zero manual steps, a good fit for his worker-finishes-then-review loop, but depends on the `hunk` tool which isn't confirmed installed.

mediums.name: hmu332233/herdr-symlink-worktree
mediums.summary: On worktree.created, symlinks configured gitignored paths (.env, build caches) from the main checkout into every new worktree, per a `.herdr-worktree-links` file.
mediums.why: Solves a real recurring pain (re-creating .env/cache per worktree) for his worktree-per-worker pattern, though it's functionally a narrower subset of freethinkel's more general worktree-hooks plugin.

mediums.name: wyattjoh/herdr-plugin-renamer
mediums.summary: On an agent's first real prompt in a numbered tab, asks an LLM (on-device Apple model, else Codex CLI) to generate a kebab-case slug and renames the tab, and — if in an auto-worktree — the git branch and workspace too.
mediums.why: Auto-naming worktrees/branches/tabs from an agent's first prompt is a nice pattern for labeling spawned team members, works off-macOS via the Codex engine (which he has), but on-device naming quality degrades on Linux.

mediums.name: AVGVSTVS96/herdr-tab-mover
mediums.summary: Moves an entire live tab — layout and running panes/agents intact, no respawn — to another workspace via an fzf overlay picker.
mediums.why: Genuinely useful for reorganizing a growing set of live agent panes across workspaces without killing running sessions, fitting his heavy multi-pane usage.

mediums.name: kakigakki/herdr-auto-namer
mediums.summary: Auto-renames agents to a short LLM (Claude haiku) summary of what they're working on (or the Claude Code session title) when a turn ends, and renames workspaces to their working-directory basename by majority vote across panes.
mediums.why: Solves real sidebar clutter from many identical 'claude' rows in a heavy multi-agent setup, but is cosmetic rather than functional, and its per-pane LLM-summary naming could conflict with whatever naming his own agent-team plugin assigns.

mediums.name: moneycaringcoder/herdr-tether
mediums.summary: Rust plugin that keeps ordinary local/SSH tmux workloads (build watchers, dev servers) alive when their Herdr view closes, with explicit stop/reopen lifecycle and a doctor/JSON diagnostics CLI.
mediums.why: Useful if he runs long build/test watchers outside Herdr's own agent-managed panes and wants them to survive closing the view, but it's not agent orchestration and doesn't touch worktrees or notifications.

mediums.name: jwarykowski/shepherd
mediums.summary: Standalone or Herdr-embedded TUI todo board backed by a plain markdown file, with categories, priorities, due/defer dates, undo, and a global cross-project view.
mediums.why: Overlaps functionally with his own file-based task_plan.md/findings.md/progress.md workflow and cavemem archive — could be a nicer front-end for the same job, but not a drop-in replacement he clearly needs.

mediums.name: KonstantinKai/herdr-harpoon
mediums.summary: Bash, zero-build Harpoon-style plugin: mark up to 5 panes per Herdr session and jump to them by index or an fzf-backed picker, with marks covering workspace+tab+pane in one jump.
mediums.why: Genuinely handy for a heavy multi-pane user, but per-session marks (not fleet-status-aware) make it a smaller win than the status-aware jump/palette tools also in this batch.

mediums.name: usrivastava92/herdr-wakeup
mediums.summary: Rust watcher that holds a system wake assertion (while allowing display sleep) whenever any Herdr agent is in the 'working' state, releasing it after a grace period.
mediums.why: Directly useful for his pattern of long unattended coordinator waits on Codex/Claude workers where the machine could otherwise sleep mid-job; supports Linux x86-64.

mediums.name: alon-z/herdr-devup
mediums.summary: Reads a per-project .herdr/dev.toml declaring tabs/panes/commands, spins up that dev-stack layout on a keypress, and can re-sync ngrok tunnel URLs into env files.
mediums.why: The declarative TOML tabs/panes-per-project model is exactly the 'declarative workspace layouts' pattern he cares about, though the tunnel-sync half is dev-server-specific and less relevant to his agent-team use case.

mediums.name: makyinmars/herdr-context.nvim
mediums.summary: Neovim plugin + Herdr companion that lets you select code/diagnostics in Neovim and stage it into a chosen live Herdr agent's prompt without submitting, with a target-agent picker overlay.
mediums.why: Editor-integration plugin capped at medium per profile (his Neovim usage is unknown), but the 'pick a live agent as addressable target' pattern is worth noting for star-topology dispatch.

mediums.name: dantehemerson/herdr-last-tab
mediums.summary: Rust plugin adding one action that toggles focus back to the previously-focused tab, tracked by stable tab ID so it survives reordering.
mediums.why: Small but genuinely useful muscle-memory tool (tmux last-window analog) for a heavy multi-tab herdr user, though narrow in scope.

mediums.name: jasonrr/herdr-tally
mediums.summary: Shared project-scoped ledger (todos, scratchpads, plans, comments) in one on-disk store, exposed via a CLI, 38 MCP tools for agents, and a Herdr-pane TUI, auto-registered with Claude Code on install.
mediums.why: Close functional overlap with his existing planning-with-files + cavemem stack for cross-session state; could be a genuine upgrade (agents read/write it directly via MCP) but isn't a clear must-switch given what he's already built.

mediums.name: haphamdev/herdr-simple-switcher
mediums.summary: fzf-backed fuzzy picker for switching between workspaces, tabs, or agents (shown with type, status, and working directory), each bound to its own key.
mediums.why: A solid general-purpose navigator for a fleet of workspaces/tabs/agents, though less targeted than the agent-state-aware jump/palette tools also in this batch.

mediums.name: dev-shimada/herdr-auto-tab-name
mediums.summary: Dependency-free TypeScript plugin that auto-labels each tab with its working directory's basename by hooking pane/tab/agent/worktree events, respecting manual renames.
mediums.why: Could reduce toil across many parallel worktree tabs, though he likely already names tabs deliberately per worker/agent role in his coordinator pattern, which may conflict with auto-labeling.

mediums.name: Phoobobo/herdr-workboard
mediums.summary: Kanban TUI plugin that maps board→workspace, column(task state)→tab, and task session→pane one-to-one; moving a card physically moves the live pane between tabs, and agent working/blocked/done state shows as a live card badge.
mediums.why: The closest existing analog to the 'run-board state' feature he's building into herdr-agent-team — worth studying closely as prior art, though he's more likely to build his own than adopt a competing implementation.

mediums.name: qq88976321/herdr-copy-search
mediums.summary: tmux-style copy mode for Herdr pane scrollback with incremental regex search, copycat-style pattern search, and extrakto-style token extraction, all via OSC 52 so it works over SSH with no remote clipboard helper.
mediums.why: Herdr's native copy mode has no scrollback search yet per the README, and searching/extracting tokens from long agent output over SSH is a real recurring need for a heavy daily user reviewing many panes.

mediums.name: alon-z/herdr-command-palette
mediums.summary: Fuzzy palette (Node/TS) that picks an open workspace or a configured project directory (with zoxide-aware priority sorting), focusing it if already open or creating a new workspace rooted there.
mediums.why: A reasonable project-launcher/workspace-switcher, complementary to devup's per-project layouts, but overshadowed by ramarivera/herdr-palette's broader scope which also covers workspaces.

mediums.name: KokiKono/herdr-kanban
mediums.summary: Terminal-native Kanban board (TODO/IN PROGRESS/DONE) persisted in SQLite; moving a task to IN PROGRESS auto-creates a linked herdr tab.
mediums.why: A working SQLite-persisted board tied to live pane state is a concrete reference for his own run-board feature, but he already has a file-based task_plan.md/progress.md system that covers similar ground.

mediums.name: bcihanc/herdr-claude-session-title
mediums.summary: Claude Code hook (SessionStart/UserPromptSubmit/Stop) that mirrors the session's /rename or auto-title into herdr pane metadata.
mediums.why: Genuinely useful for identifying many concurrent Claude worker panes at a glance without manual naming, though it's pure QoL rather than orchestration.

mediums.name: JacquesvanWyk/herdr-lazygit
mediums.summary: Toggleable lazygit in a herdr split pane or dedicated tab (open/focus/close).
mediums.why: Solid git-heavy-user QoL, especially useful across his many worktrees, but not central to orchestration.

mediums.name: ycros/herdr-compass
mediums.summary: Unified directional (h/j/k/l-style) navigation across panes, tabs, and workspaces with wraparound.
mediums.why: Useful ergonomics for someone juggling many panes/workspaces daily, but purely a navigation convenience.

mediums.name: arjenblokzijl/herdr-launcher
mediums.summary: Ratatui TUI + CLI launcher that runs declarative TOML workflows with a fill-in field form (text/multiline/fuzzy-select), ships as a single static Rust binary.
mediums.why: The declarative-workflow-plus-form-launch pattern is a reusable idea for a spawn-agent-variant picker in his own plugin, though as shipped it's a generic command launcher, not agent-aware.

mediums.name: nicolasvasquez/herdr-smart-workspace
mediums.summary: fzf overlay picker to switch between existing herdr workspaces or launch new ones from zoxide paths.
mediums.why: Handy workspace-switching QoL for someone managing many project workspaces, but not orchestration-specific.

mediums.name: carsonjones/herdr-agent-dashboard
mediums.summary: Simple real-time bun/TSX dashboard showing all herdr agents, launchable as a plugin pane or standalone script.
mediums.why: A minimal but functional fleet-monitoring dashboard aligned with his 'monitoring agent fleets' interest; less featureful than building his own run-board but usable as-is or as a reference.

mediums.name: wilbeibi/herdr-catchup
mediums.summary: Reads local agent session history (Codex/Claude/OpenCode/Pi) and lets you summarize, natively fork, or hand off a session's full transcript to a different agent CLI in a new pane.
mediums.why: Cross-agent context handoff is conceptually close to his file-based brief/report protocol, but it's a situational feature (hitting usage limits or deliberately switching agents), not weekly-driver material.

mediums.name: iurysza/termscope
mediums.summary: Scans the active pane's visible viewport for real files/URLs and opens the selected one (nvim for files, browser for links) via an fzf-style picker.
mediums.why: Useful when agents dump stack traces/paths, but core file-open action defaults to nvim whose usage by this user is unconfirmed, so capped per profile guidance on editor-integration plugins.

mediums.name: juninaba/herdr-slack-notify
mediums.summary: Zero-dependency Node script that posts a Slack message via incoming webhook when an agent reaches done or blocked.
mediums.why: The done/blocked-triggered webhook pattern is a direct analog to a status-push mechanism, but there's no evidence he uses Slack day-to-day, making it situational rather than a clear fit.

mediums.name: qdentity/herdr-worktree-lifecycle
mediums.summary: Dispatches worktree.created/opened/removed events to repo-committed setup/teardown wrapper scripts, with per-worktree serialization, status markers, logs, and a completion notification.
mediums.why: A more complete (setup+teardown) worktree automation than the provisioner plugin, but requires each repo to commit its own wrapper scripts under a fixed ABI — more invasive across his many varied projects than the config-driven provisioner.

mediums.name: langtind/gren-herdr
mediums.summary: Listens for herdr's worktree.created event and runs gren's post-create setup (env symlink, deps install, direnv, lifecycle hooks) inside the new worktree's own pane with a real TTY; also ships an fzf create/switch/remove picker.
mediums.why: The worktree.created event-hook + pane-injected setup pattern is directly reusable for his worktree fan-out pre-flight in herdr-agent-team, though it requires adopting the gren CLI he doesn't currently use.

mediums.name: kbrdn1/herdr-plugin-gwm
mediums.summary: Drives the gwm CLI for worktree create/switch/remove/PR-review/exec-across-worktrees/clean, with herdr adopting (never owning) the resulting worktrees, plus a live gwm dashboard TUI.
mediums.why: The adopt-not-own worktree model, exec-across-worktrees, and live dashboard are solid precedent for run-board and worktree-fanout mechanics in herdr-agent-team, but it's tied to the gwm CLI he doesn't use.

mediums.name: ditwrd/herdr-remote-worktrunk
mediums.summary: fzf-driven flow (fork of herdr-worktrunk) that turns picking a GitHub issue, remote branch, or PR into a worktree + herdr workspace via the worktrunk CLI.
mediums.why: Issue/PR-to-worktree automation touches both his GitHub-flow and worktree-ergonomics interests, though it needs the worktrunk tool and is a fairly thin fzf wrapper.

mediums.name: willfish/herdr-navigator
mediums.summary: vim-tmux-navigator equivalent for herdr: Alt+hjkl forwards to Vim/Neovim inside the active pane, otherwise moves herdr pane focus.
mediums.why: Standard editor-integration plugin; his Vim/Neovim usage is unknown so capped at medium per profile guidance.

mediums.name: lmilojevicc/herdr-tab-rename
mediums.summary: Daemon that auto-renames each tab to its focused pane's cwd basename, honoring manual renames once set.
mediums.why: Small but genuine QoL win for someone running many concurrent worktree panes/tabs and needing to tell them apart at a glance.

mediums.name: juninaba/herdr-pr-preview
mediums.summary: Right-side pane showing the current branch's GitHub PR with live CI-check status, auto-refreshing while checks run.
mediums.why: Useful for watching his own PR's CI, but it's a narrow single-branch slice of what herdr-plugin-github-dash already covers more fully.

mediums.name: ramarivera/herdr-pretty-which
mediums.summary: Rust/Ratatui which-key style overlay that reads his real herdr config and renders a searchable, filterable keybinding guide (default/config/unset).
mediums.why: As a heavy daily herdr user who'd be layering several more plugin keybindings on top of his own, a live cheat-sheet has real recurring value.

mediums.name: willfish/herdr-workspacex
mediums.summary: Rust-native fuzzy workspace switcher; typing an unknown name creates a new workspace at the zoxide-resolved directory (or falls back to $HOME/<query>).
mediums.why: Fast switch/create loop is a genuine speed win for someone juggling many project checkouts daily, though it's convenience rather than orchestration.

mediums.name: mvallebr/herdr-telegram-plugin
mediums.summary: Maps each herdr agent pane 1:1 to a Telegram forum topic; messages in the topic are forwarded as keyboard input to the pane, agent output is sent back — zero LLM in the path.
mediums.why: Hits the same mobile remote-control space as the Telegram/Slack bridge but is Telegram-only and less mature (no blocked-agent-specific enrichment/ticketing), so it's a secondary option to that higher-fit plugin.

mediums.name: Javamomma/herdr-matter-wall
mediums.summary: Opens the most-active subdirectories of a project as a live wall of read-only status cards, one small strictly-read-only Claude agent per subdirectory summarizing status into a severity-coded card, one full-screen tab each.
mediums.why: Not literally fleet monitoring, but its structured status-card rendering, severity coding, and per-item tab layout are a reusable pattern for a run-board display in herdr-agent-team.

mediums.name: nicolegros/herdr-launcher
mediums.summary: Fuzzy directory picker that creates or switches to a workspace, with duplicate detection and an on_create hook to run a shell script (e.g. scripted pane splits/layout) after creation.
mediums.why: The on_create layout-scripting hook brushes against his 'declarative workspace layouts' interest, though it's an imperative script hook rather than a declarative layout system.

mediums.name: Newt6611/herdr-plugin-rust
mediums.summary: Published Rust crate (herdr-plugin) providing an App/Context/event-handler framework (setup, on_event, typed config) for building herdr plugins.
mediums.why: Not a consumer feature but the most direct scaffolding available for actually building herdr-agent-team in Rust — worth evaluating as a dependency even though it isn't something he'd 'install' weekly.
